fields.name: History
sections: The End of the Cold War

cards.back: The emergence of the first writing systems — cuneiform wedge-marks on clay in Sumer, with Egyptian hieroglyphs appearing soon after — turning spoken language into durable records.
cards.extra:
Writing began as bookkeeping in Sumer's temple economies and became civilisation's storage medium — law, contracts, scripture, history itself. It is the line between 'prehistory' and 'history': everything we know in the ancients' own words starts here.

Region: Mesopotamia

cards.back: Narmer was an ancient Egyptian king of the Early Dynastic Period, whose reign began at the end of the 4th millennium BC.
cards.extra:
Narmer's union of Upper and Lower Egypt created history's first large territorial state — and its most durable: pharaonic civilisation ran, recognisably itself, for some three thousand years on the rhythm of the Nile flood.

Region: Egypt

cards.back: The Indus Valley Civilisation (IVC), also known as the Indus Civilisation, was a Bronze Age civilisation in the northwestern regions of South Asia, lasting from 3300 BCE to 1300 BCE, and in its mature form from 2600 BCE to 1900 BCE.
cards.extra:
The third great river civilisation, and the largest in area. Mohenjo-daro and Harappa had planned street grids and sanitation millennia ahead of most of the world — yet its script is undeciphered, so one cradle of civilisation still cannot speak to us.

Region: South Asia

cards.back: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the largest of the Egyptian pyramids and the most famous landmark of the Giza pyramid complex in Giza, Egypt.
cards.extra:
Khufu's tomb is the measure of what the early state could already organise: tens of thousands of workers, fed, housed and coordinated for decades. It stayed the tallest human-made structure for nearly four millennia and is the only one of the Seven Wonders still standing.

Region: Egypt

cards.back: The Code of Hammurabi is a Babylonian legal text composed c.
cards.extra:
The most complete early law code, carved in stone and set up in public: justice as something written, knowable and issued by the state rather than settled by private vengeance. Its 'eye for an eye' began life as a *limit* on retaliation, not a licence.

Region: Mesopotamia

cards.back: The Late Bronze Age collapse was a period of societal collapse in the Mediterranean basin during the late 13th to early 12th century BC.
cards.extra:
The first known collapse of a connected international system: in roughly fifty years the Hittite empire fell, Mycenaean Greece and the Levantine trade cities burned, and Egypt barely held on — causes still debated ('Sea Peoples', drought, systems failure). Out of the dark age that followed came iron tools and the alphabet.

Region: Eastern Mediterranean

cards.back: The Olmecs or Olmec were an early major Mesoamerican civilization, flourishing in the modern-day Mexican states of Veracruz and Tabasco from roughly 1200 to 400 BC during Mesoamerica's formative period.
cards.extra:
Mesoamerica's first major civilisation, famous for its colossal basalt heads. It set the templates — pyramid-and-plaza cities, the ritual calendar, the ballgame — that the Maya and Aztecs inherited, and it proves civilisation arose in the Americas entirely independently of Eurasia.

Region: Mesoamerica

cards.back: The Kingdom of Kush, also known as the Kushite Empire, or simply Kush, was an ancient kingdom in Nubia, centered along the Nile Valley in what is now Sudan.
cards.extra:
Egypt's great rival on the middle Nile: for nearly a century its kings ruled Egypt as the 25th Dynasty, and its later capital Meroë ran one of the ancient world's great iron industries. A standing correction to the idea that ancient African power ended at Egypt.

Region: Nubia / NE Africa

cards.back: The Phoenician alphabet is an abjad that was used across the Mediterranean civilization of Phoenicia for most of the 1st millennium BC.
cards.extra:
By reducing writing from hundreds of signs to ~22 letters, Phoenician traders democratised literacy — and their alphabet, carried port to port, became the ancestor of nearly every alphabet on Earth: Greek and Latin, Hebrew and Arabic, Cyrillic.

Region: Levant

cards.back: The ancient Olympic Games, or the ancient Olympics, were a series of athletic competitions among representatives of city-states and one of the Panhellenic Games of ancient Greece.
cards.extra:
The games at Olympia gave the fractious Greek world a shared festival, a sacred truce and even a common calendar (Greeks dated years by Olympiads) — evidence of a shared 'Greekness' above the endlessly warring city-states.

Region: Greece

cards.back: The Babylonian captivity or Babylonian exile was the period in Jewish history during which a large number of Judeans from the ancient Kingdom of Judah were exiled to Babylonia by the Neo-Babylonian Empire.
cards.extra:
Nebuchadnezzar II destroyed Solomon's Temple and deported Judah's elite to Babylon. In exile, Judaism reshaped itself around text and law instead of temple and place — a portable faith, which shaped Judaism permanently and, through it, Christianity and Islam. (Cyrus of Persia freed the exiles in 538.)

Region: Mesopotamia / Levant

cards.front: Founding of the Persian Empire (550 BCE)
cards.back: The Achaemenid Empire was an ancient Iranian empire founded by Cyrus the Great of the Achaemenid dynasty in 550 BC.
cards.extra:
Cyrus the Great's Achaemenid Empire was history's first superpower — from the Aegean to the Indus, organised into satrapies, linked by royal roads, and ruling dozens of peoples with pragmatic tolerance. It set the imperial template every later empire studied.

Region: Persia

cards.front: Founding of the Roman Republic (509 BCE)
cards.back: The overthrow of Rome's monarchy (traditionally 509 BCE, expelling Tarquin the Proud) and its replacement with annually elected consuls and a senate.
cards.extra:
Rome replaced its kings with elected magistrates, a senate and term limits — institutions designed to prevent one-man rule. That republic (and the story of its failure) became the reference point for every later one, from Venice to the American founders.

Region: Rome

cards.back: Athenian democracy developed around the 6th century BC in the Greek city-state of Athens, comprising the city of Athens and the surrounding territory of Attica, and focusing on supporting liberty, equality, and security.
cards.extra:
Cleisthenes' reforms made the assembly of citizens sovereign in Athens — the first known democracy. Direct, male-only and slave-holding, yet the proof of concept that ordinary citizens could govern; the golden age of Greek drama and philosophy unfolded under it.

Region: Greece

cards.back: The Greco-Persian Wars, also called the Persian Wars, were a series of armed conflicts involving various Greek city-states and the Achaemenid Empire from 499 BC to 449 BC.
cards.extra:
The Greek city-states — above all Athens and Sparta — twice repelled the Persian superpower. Victory bought classical Greece its golden age (and Athens its empire); had it gone the other way, the culture at the root of Western philosophy, drama and politics might have been a Persian province.

Region: Greece / Persia

cards.back: The emergence of the Buddha's teaching (the dharma) in the Ganges plain of northern India, and its growth into a monastic, missionary religion.
cards.extra:
Siddhartha Gautama's teaching — suffering, its cause, and the discipline that ends it — became the first great missionary religion, carried along the Silk Road to Central Asia, China, Japan and Southeast Asia: the first faith to span whole civilisations.

Region: South Asia

cards.back: The Peloponnesian War was fought in the Eastern Mediterranean between the Athenian-led Delian League and the Spartan-led Peloponnesian League from 431 to 404 BC for hegemony over Ancient Greece.
cards.extra:
Athens and Sparta fought the Greek world to exhaustion; Athens fell, and no city-state was left strong enough to resist Macedon two generations later. Thucydides' account — power, fear and interest — founded political realism, and the 'Thucydides trap' still names the danger when a rising power meets a ruling one.

Region: Greece

cards.front: Conquests of Alexander the Great (334–323 BCE)
cards.back: Alexander the Great of Macedon carried out a series of conquests and military campaigns from 336 to 323 BC.
cards.extra:
Alexander destroyed the Persian Empire and died at 32 ruling from Greece to the Indus. The conquest fused Greek and Eastern worlds into the Hellenistic age — Greek as the lingua franca, Alexandria as the knowledge capital — the channel through which Greek thought reached Rome and, much later, us.

Region: Greece → Egypt → India

cards.front: Founding of the Maurya Empire (c. 321 BCE)
cards.back: The Maurya Empire was a geographically extensive Iron Age historical power in South Asia with its power base in Magadha.
cards.extra:
Chandragupta Maurya seized Magadha and, in the power vacuum Alexander left in the northwest, built the first empire spanning nearly all of India — the state his grandson Ashoka would rule when he renounced war and sent Buddhism across Asia.

Region: South Asia

cards.back: The Punic Wars were a series of wars fought between the Roman Republic and the Carthaginian Empire during the period 264 to 146 BC.
cards.extra:
Rome's three-round struggle with Carthage — surviving Hannibal's invasion of Italy in the second — ended with Carthage erased and Rome the unrivalled Mediterranean power: the springboard from Italian republic to world empire.

Region: Mediterranean

cards.back: Qin's wars of unification were a series of military campaigns launched in the late 3rd century BC by the state of Qin against the other six states remaining in China – Han, Zhao, Yan, Wei, Chu and Qi.
cards.extra:
Qin Shi Huang ended the Warring States and created the first unified China — one script, one currency, one law. The centralised imperial model he built survived, dynasty after dynasty, until 1912: 'China' as a single state starts here.

Region: China

cards.front: Opening of the Silk Road (c. 130 BCE)
cards.back: The opening of the overland trade routes linking Han China with Central Asia, Persia and the Mediterranean, following Zhang Qian's diplomatic missions west.
cards.extra:
Zhang Qian's missions for Han China opened the relay routes that moved silk, horses, faiths and plagues across Eurasia for 1,500 years. Buddhism travelled east along it; Rome and Han China — who never met — traded through its middlemen. Globalisation's first draft.

Region: Eurasia

cards.back: Julius Caesar, the Roman dictator, was assassinated on the Ides of March, 44 BC, by a group of senators during a Senate session at the Curia of Pompey, located within the Theatre of Pompey in Rome.
cards.extra:
The senators killed Caesar to save the republic and destroyed it instead: the civil wars that followed ended with his heir Octavian as sole master of Rome. The classic lesson that removing a strongman does not restore the institutions he broke.

Region: Rome

cards.front: Augustus founds the Roman Empire (27 BCE)
cards.back: The Roman Empire was a state that dominated the Mediterranean and much of Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa during the classical period.
cards.extra:
Augustus turned the republic into a de-facto monarchy while keeping its forms, and opened the Pax Romana — two centuries of Mediterranean peace under one state whose law, roads, cities and Latin became the foundations of Europe.

Region: Mediterranean

cards.back: Jesus was put to death by crucifixion, being nailed to an instrument made by the Romans for capital punishment, commonly named as a cross.
cards.extra:
From this execution — and his followers' faith in his resurrection — grew Christianity, today the world's largest religion (~2.3 billion). Within three centuries the empire that crucified him had adopted his faith; no single event shaped Western civilisation more deeply.

Region: Roman Judaea

cards.back: The development of paper-making in Han China — pulped plant fibre pressed into cheap sheets — traditionally credited to the court official Cai Lun.
cards.extra:
Cai Lun's paper made the written word cheap. It carried Chinese bureaucracy and scholarship, travelled west along the Silk Road (reaching Europe over a thousand years later), and made mass literacy — and eventually printing — possible: knowledge's great cost collapse before Gutenberg.

Region: China

cards.back: The Edict of Milan was the 13 February 313 AD agreement to treat Christians benevolently within the Roman Empire.
cards.extra:
Constantine's edict ended the persecution of Christians; within decades the outlawed sect became the empire's official religion (380). The fusion of Roman state and Christian church shaped Europe, Byzantium and the papacy — and his new capital, Constantinople (330), guarded it for the next thousand years.

Region: Rome

cards.front: Fall of the Western Roman Empire (476 CE)
cards.back: The fall of the Western Roman Empire, also called the fall of the Roman Empire or the fall of Rome, was the loss of central political control in the Western Roman Empire, a process in which the Empire failed to enforce its rule, and its vast territory was divided among several successor polities.
cards.extra:
The western empire dissolved into Germanic kingdoms — the conventional end of antiquity and start of Europe's Middle Ages. But Rome only half-fell: the eastern half, Byzantium, carried Roman law and Greek learning for another thousand years, until 1453.

Region: Europe

time.title: Fall of the Western Roman Empire

cards.back: The Corpus Juris Civilis is the modern name for a collection of fundamental works in jurisprudence, enacted from 529 to 534 by order of Roman Emperor Justinian I.
cards.extra:
Justinian's jurists compressed a thousand years of Roman law into one body of authority. Rediscovered in Italy centuries later, it became the foundation of continental Europe's civil-law systems — most of the world's law today descends from this code or from England's rival common law.

Region: Byzantium

cards.back: The Tang dynasty was an imperial dynasty of China that ruled from 618 to 907, with an interregnum between 690 and 705.
cards.extra:
Under the Tang, China was the world's most powerful and open civilisation — Chang'an hosted merchants and faiths from across Eurasia, poetry reached its classical peak, and Chinese institutions were copied by Japan, Korea and Vietnam. The benchmark East Asia has looked back to ever since.

Region: East Asia

cards.back: The Hijrah, also Hegira, was the journey the Islamic prophet Muhammad and his followers took from Mecca to Medina.
cards.extra:
Muhammad's migration to Medina turned a persecuted movement into a self-governing community — the founding moment of Islam (the Muslim calendar counts from it). Within a century, the faith he preached stretched from Spain to Central Asia; today it is the faith of about two billion people.

Region: Middle East

cards.back: The early Muslim conquests or early Islamic conquests, also known as the Arab conquests, were a series of religious wars initiated by Muhammad, the prophet of Islam, and continued by the early Muslims.
cards.extra:
One of history's fastest expansions: Arab armies destroyed Persia outright, took Byzantium's richest provinces (Syria, Egypt, North Africa), and reached Spain and the Indus within 120 years. The Middle East and North Africa have been predominantly Muslim and largely Arabic-speaking ever since.

Region: Middle East

cards.back: Polynesian navigation or Polynesian wayfinding was used for thousands of years to enable long voyages across thousands of kilometres of the open Pacific Ocean.
cards.extra:
In double-hulled canoes, navigating by stars, swells and birds, Polynesians settled the Pacific from Hawai'i to Easter Island to New Zealand (c. 1250) — the last major habitable lands humans reached, and the greatest feat of navigation before the satellite.

Region: Pacific

cards.back: Al-Andalus was the Muslim-ruled area of the Iberian Peninsula.
cards.extra:
Muslim Iberia — with Córdoba at its peak among the largest, most learned cities on Earth — was the main gateway through which Greek and Arabic science re-entered Europe. The Christian Reconquista ended it at Granada in 1492, the same year Columbus sailed: one Spain closed as another opened.

Region: Europe

cards.back: The Viking Age was the period during the Middle Ages when Norsemen known as Vikings undertook large-scale raiding, colonising, conquest, and trading throughout Europe and reached North America.
cards.extra:
Scandinavian raiders-turned-settlers reshaped the map: they founded Normandy and the Danelaw, opened the river routes that became the Rus (Russia's very name), settled Iceland, and reached North America (Vinland, c. 1000) five centuries before Columbus.

Region: Europe

cards.back: Charlemagne was King of the Franks from 768, King of the Lombards from 774, and Emperor of what is now known as the Carolingian Empire from 800.
cards.extra:
Three centuries after Rome's fall, the pope crowned a Frankish king emperor — reviving the imperial idea in the West, binding church to crown, and founding the realm whose later split became France and Germany. Europe's medieval order starts here.

Region: Europe

cards.back: The House of Wisdom, also known as the Grand Library of Baghdad, was believed to be a major Abbasid-era public academy and intellectual center in Baghdad.
cards.extra:
At the Abbasid court, Greek, Persian and Indian learning was translated, preserved and pushed forward — algebra and 'algorithm' carry the name of its scholar al-Khwarizmi, and 'Arabic' numerals arrived from India through it. Much of antiquity survived to reach Europe because Baghdad kept it alive.

Region: Middle East

cards.back: The Song dynasty was an imperial dynasty of China that ruled from 960 to 1279.
cards.extra:
Song China ran the most advanced economy on Earth: the first paper money, gunpowder weapons, the magnetic compass at sea, mass woodblock printing, cities of a million people. Three of the inventions Europe later used to conquer the world were Chinese first.

Region: East Asia

cards.back: The Christianization of Kievan Rus' was a long and complicated process that took place in several stages.
cards.extra:
Vladimir of Kyiv took his faith from Constantinople, not Rome — orienting the future Russia and Ukraine toward Orthodoxy, the Cyrillic alphabet and the Byzantine idea of empire. The civilisational line between Orthodox East and Catholic West still runs where this decision drew it.

Region: Russia

cards.back: The East–West Schism, also known as the Great Schism or the Schism of 1054, is the break of communion between the Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church since 1054.
cards.extra:
Centuries of drift between Latin West and Greek East ended in mutual excommunication: Catholic and Orthodox Christianity have been separate ever since. The border between them — through the Balkans and Ukraine — remains one of Europe's deepest cultural fault lines.

Region: Europe

cards.back: The Norman Conquest of England was an 11th-century invasion by an army made up of thousands of Norman, French, Flemish, and Breton troops, all led by the Duke of Normandy, later styled William the Conqueror.
cards.extra:
William's victory at Hastings rewired England: a French-speaking aristocracy (which is why English is a Germanic language soaked in French), strong central kingship, and a cross-Channel entanglement that shaped both England and France for four centuries.

Region: Europe

cards.back: A medieval university was a corporation organized during the Middle Ages for the purposes of higher education.
cards.extra:
Europe invented a self-governing corporation dedicated to teaching and disputation — degrees, faculties, exams. The university proved the most durable European institution after the church itself, and became the engine room of the scientific revolution to come.

Region: Europe

cards.back: The Crusades were a series of military campaigns launched by the papacy between 1095 and 1291 against Muslim rulers for the recovery and defence of the Holy Land, as part of a wider crusading movement.
cards.extra:
Rome's call to take Jerusalem launched two centuries of holy war. The crusader states fell in the end, but the collision transformed both sides: Italian ports grew rich on the traffic, East–West contact deepened — and the memory still colours relations between the West and the Middle East.

Region: Middle East

cards.back: Great Zimbabwe was a city in the south-eastern hills of the modern country of Zimbabwe, near Masvingo.
cards.extra:
The granite-walled capital of a Shona kingdom grown rich on gold and Indian Ocean trade — its finds include Chinese porcelain and Persian glass. The largest ancient structure south of the Sahara, it gave modern Zimbabwe its name.

Region: Southern Africa

cards.back: Angkor Wat is a Vaishnava Hindu and Theravada Buddhist temple complex in Siem Reap, Cambodia, and the largest religious complex in the world.
cards.extra:
The temple-city of the Khmer Empire — first Hindu, later Buddhist — crowned the greatest power of medieval Southeast Asia, an empire of reservoirs and rice that fed a metropolis of perhaps a million people. It remains the largest religious structure ever built, and Cambodia's national symbol.

Region: Southeast Asia

cards.back: The Kamakura shogunate  was the feudal military government of Japan during the Kamakura period from 1185 to 1333.
cards.extra:
Japan's first shogunate put real power in the hands of a warrior class while the emperor reigned as a sacred figurehead — the template of samurai rule that lasted, in one form or another, until 1868. Japan's distinctive dual order starts here.

Region: Japan

cards.back: The Delhi Sultanate or the Sultanate of Delhi was a late medieval empire primarily based in Delhi that stretched over large parts of the Indian subcontinent for more than three centuries.
cards.extra:
Turkic and Afghan dynasties ruling from Delhi made Islam a lasting political and cultural force in South Asia — the layering of Muslim rule over Hindu society that the Mughals deepened, the British exploited, and the 1947 Partition finally, violently unwound.

Region: South Asia

cards.back: The Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous empire in history.
cards.extra:
From the steppe, Genghis Khan and his heirs conquered from Korea to Hungary — history's largest contiguous empire. Destroyer and connector at once: Baghdad's sack (1258) ended the Islamic golden age, yet the Pax Mongolica reopened Eurasia's roads for traders like Marco Polo — and for the plague.

Region: Eurasia

cards.back: Magna Carta, sometimes spelled Magna Charta, is a royal charter of rights sealed by King John of England at Runnymede, near Windsor, on 15 June 1215.
cards.extra:
A failed peace treaty that became a founding myth: the principle that even the king is bound by law and cannot tax or imprison at will. Its language echoes through the English constitution, the American Bill of Rights and modern due process.

Region: Europe

cards.back: Mansa Musa was the ninth Mansa of the Mali Empire, which reached its territorial peak during his reign.
cards.extra:
Mansa Musa's Mali controlled the gold and salt roads across the Sahara; his spectacular pilgrimage to Mecca put West African wealth on the world's maps — literally. Timbuktu under Mali became a centre of Islamic scholarship, a standing rebuttal to the myth of an Africa outside history.

Region: West Africa

cards.back: The Aztec Empire, also known as the Triple Alliance or historiographically as the Tenochca Empire but most accurately known as the Mexica, was an alliance of three Nahua city-states: Mexico-Tenochtitlan, Tetzcoco, and Tlacopan.
cards.extra:
From Tenochtitlan, a canal-laced island city bigger than most in Europe, the Aztecs built Mesoamerica's last great empire of tribute and ritual. Its wealth — and its resentful subject peoples — made it the first target of Spain's American conquest (1521).

Region: Americas

cards.back: The Black Death was a plague pandemic that occurred in Europe from 1346 to 1353.
cards.extra:
Plague travelled the reopened Eurasian trade routes and killed a third to half of Europe in four years — history's deadliest pandemic. The survivors' labour was suddenly scarce and valuable: serfdom cracked, wages rose, and the old feudal order never fully recovered.

Region: Europe

cards.back: The Ming treasure voyages were maritime expeditions undertaken by Ming China's treasure fleet between 1405 and 1433.
cards.extra:
Decades before Portugal rounded Africa, Ming China sent fleets dwarfing anything in Europe as far as Arabia and East Africa — then abruptly turned inward and dismantled them. History's great 'what if': the oceans were left to Europe's small, hungry, competing kingdoms.

Region: East Asia

cards.back: The Inca Empire, officially known as the Realm of the Four Parts, was the largest empire in pre-Columbian America.
cards.extra:
In a single century the Incas knit the Andes into the largest empire of the pre-Columbian Americas — terraced mountains, 40,000 km of roads, knotted-cord records (quipu) — all without wheels, iron or writing. Proof of how differently state power can be built; Spain took it in 1533.

Region: Americas

cards.back: A printing press is a mechanical machine that transfers ink onto materials such as paper or cloth by applying pressure to an inked surface.
cards.extra:
Gutenberg's press collapsed the cost of the written word: more books in the fifty years after it than in the previous thousand. Without it, no Reformation, no scientific journals, no newspapers — the information revolution that made every later one possible.

Region: Europe

cards.back: The Fall of Constantinople, also known as the Conquest of Constantinople, was the capture of Constantinople, the capital city of the Byzantine Empire, by the Ottoman Empire.
cards.extra:
Mehmed II's cannon ended the Roman Empire's last remnant, a thousand years after the West fell. The Ottomans became the great power of the eastern Mediterranean; Greek scholars fleeing west fed the Renaissance; and with the old routes east now taxed by a rival, Atlantic Europe went looking for another way to Asia.

Region: Middle East

cards.back:
Between 1492 and 1504, the Italian explorer and navigator Christopher Columbus
led four Spanish transatlantic maritime expeditions in the name of the Catholic Monarchs to the Americas.
cards.extra:
Columbus, sailing for Spain, connected two halves of the planet that had evolved apart for millennia. The Columbian exchange that followed — crops, silver, peoples, empires and diseases that killed most of the Americas' population — is the true hinge between the medieval and modern worlds.

Region: Global

cards.back: World War I, or the First World War, also known as the Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Central Powers.
cards.extra:
The first industrial-scale total war (~20 million dead). It toppled four empires — German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman and Russian — redrew the map of Europe and the Middle East, and, through a punitive peace, set the stage for WWII.

Region: Europe / Global

cards.back: The Russian Revolution was a period of political and social change in Russia, starting in 1917.
cards.extra:
Brought the Bolsheviks to power and created the world's first communist state (the USSR). It split the 20th century into a capitalist–communist contest that would become the Cold War.

Region: Russia

cards.back: The Treaty of Versailles was a peace treaty signed on 28 June 1919.
cards.extra:
Ended WWI and imposed harsh terms on Germany — territorial losses, heavy reparations, and the 'war-guilt' clause. Its severity is widely argued to have fed German grievance and the rise of Nazism (a cautionary tale about punitive peace).

Region: Europe

cards.back: The Great Depression was a severe global economic downturn from 1929 to 1939.
cards.extra:
The worst economic slump in modern history. Mass unemployment and despair fed political extremism — above all the rise of the Nazis in Germany — and helped pave the road to WWII.

Region: Global

cards.back: World War II, or the Second World War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers.
cards.extra:
The deadliest conflict in history (~70–85 million dead), including the Holocaust. It ended European global dominance, opened the nuclear age, and left the United States and the USSR as rival superpowers.

Region: Global

cards.back: The Holocaust, known in Hebrew as the Shoah, was the genocide of European Jews during World War II.
cards.extra:
The systematic murder of about six million Jews — and millions of Roma, disabled people and others — by Nazi Germany. The defining moral catastrophe of the century, it drove the founding of Israel, the UN Genocide Convention and the concept of 'crimes against humanity'.

Region: Europe

cards.back: The Bretton Woods Conference, formally known as the United Nations Monetary and Financial Conference, was the gathering of 730 delegates from all 44 allied nations at the Mount Washington Hotel, in Bretton Woods, New Hampshire, United States, to regulate what would be the international monetary and financial order…
cards.extra:
Designed the post-war economic order: it created the IMF and the World Bank and a system of fixed exchange rates anchored to the US dollar — making the dollar the world's reserve currency. (The gold link ended in 1971, but the IMF and World Bank endure.)

Region: Global

cards.back: The United Nations (UN) is a global intergovernmental organization established by the signing of the Charter of the United Nations on 26 June 1945 with the articulated mission of maintaining international peace and security, to develop friendly relations among states, to promote international cooperation, and to serve…
cards.extra:
Replaced the failed League of Nations as the forum for collective security and cooperation. Its Security Council's five permanent members (US, UK, France, Russia, China) hold vetoes to this day.

Region: Global

cards.front: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki (1945)
cards.back: On 6 and 9 August 1945, the United States detonated two atomic bombs over the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, respectively, during the final days of World War II.
cards.extra:
US atomic bombs destroyed two Japanese cities, killing ~200,000 and forcing Japan's surrender to end WWII. They opened the nuclear age — and the deterrence, arms race and existential fear that defined the Cold War.

Region: Japan

time.title: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki

cards.back: The partition of India in 1947 was the division of British India into two independent dominion states, the Union of India and Dominion of Pakistan.
cards.extra:
Split British India into India and Pakistan, triggering one of history's largest migrations (~15 million people) and mass communal violence (~1 million+ deaths). It created the enduring India–Pakistan rivalry and the Kashmir dispute.

Region: South Asia

cards.back: The Marshall Plan was an American initiative enacted in 1948 to provide foreign aid to Western Europe.
cards.extra:
Over $13 billion of US aid revived Western Europe's economies, tied them to the United States and helped contain communism — the template for economic power as grand strategy.

Region: Europe

cards.back: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) is a document adopted by the United Nations General Assembly that codifies some of the rights and freedoms of all human beings.
cards.extra:
Adopted in the shadow of the Holocaust, it set out for the first time a common global standard of fundamental rights — the moral backbone of modern international law.

Region: Global

cards.back: The Israeli Declaration of Independence, formally the Declaration of the Establishment of the State of Israel, was proclaimed at the Tel Aviv Museum on 14 May 1948, at the end of the civil war phase and beginning of the Arab–Israeli War of the 1948 Palestine war, by the Va'ad Leumi led by David Ben-Gurion, the…
cards.extra:
Created the modern State of Israel, triggering the first Arab–Israeli war and the Palestinian 'Nakba' (mass displacement). It set off the Arab–Israeli and Israeli–Palestinian conflicts that still shape the Middle East.

Region: Middle East

cards.back: The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) is an intergovernmental military alliance between 32 member states.
cards.extra:
Bound North America and Western Europe in mutual defence (an attack on one is an attack on all), hardening the Cold War divide; the USSR replied with the Warsaw Pact in 1955.

Region: Europe / N. America

cards.back: The Chinese Communist Revolution was a social and political revolution in mainland China that began in 1927 and culminated with the proclamation of the People's Republic of China (PRC) in 1949.
cards.extra:
Mao Zedong's Communists won the civil war and founded the People's Republic, bringing a fifth of humanity under communist rule — a second communist giant that reshaped the Cold War and set up modern China.

Region: East Asia

cards.back: The Korean War was an armed conflict fought on the Korean Peninsula between North Korea and South Korea and their allies.
cards.extra:
Communist North Korea, backed by China and the USSR, invaded the US-backed South; three years of war ended in stalemate at the 38th parallel — a division, and an unfinished war, that endure today.

Region: East Asia

cards.back: The Vietnam War was an armed conflict in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia fought between North Vietnam and South Vietnam and their allies.
cards.extra:
A Cold War proxy war in which communist North Vietnam defeated the US and reunified the country — America's first major military defeat, which scarred its politics and exposed the limits of superpower force against a determined insurgency.

Region: Southeast Asia

cards.back: The Suez Crisis, also known as the second Arab–Israeli war, the Tripartite Aggression in the Arab world and the Sinai War in Israel, was a British–French–Israeli invasion of Egypt in 1956.
cards.extra:
Egypt's Nasser nationalised the Suez Canal; a British–French–Israeli invasion was humiliatingly reversed under US and Soviet pressure. It marked the end of Britain and France as great imperial powers and confirmed the two superpowers as the world's arbiters.

Region: Middle East

cards.back: The Cuban Revolution was the military and political movement that overthrew the dictatorship of Fulgencio Batista, who had ruled Cuba from 1952 to 1959.
cards.extra:
Fidel Castro's revolution turned Cuba communist on the United States' doorstep — leading to the US embargo, the Bay of Pigs, and in 1962 the Cuban Missile Crisis.

Region: Americas

cards.back: The Year of Africa refers to a series of events that took place during the year 1960—mainly the independence of seventeen African nations—that highlighted the growing pan-African sentiments in the continent.
cards.extra:
In one year, 17 African countries won independence from European empires — the high-water mark of decolonisation, which remade the world map and transformed the UN's membership.

Region: Africa

cards.back: The Cuban Missile Crisis, also known as the October Crisis in Cuba, or the Caribbean Crisis, was a 13-day confrontation between the governments of the United States and the Soviet Union, when American deployments of nuclear missiles in the United Kingdom, Italy and Turkey were matched by Soviet deployments of nuclear…
cards.extra:
The closest the world came to nuclear war — a 13-day standoff over Soviet missiles in Cuba. Its peaceful resolution produced the Moscow–Washington hotline and nudged the superpowers toward détente.

Region: Americas

cards.back: Apollo 11 was the American spaceflight that first landed humans on the Moon, and the fifth crewed mission of NASA's Apollo program.
cards.extra:
The first crewed Moon landing — the climax of the US–Soviet Space Race, and proof that Cold War rivalry drove the century's greatest technological leaps.

Region: Global

cards.back: Reform and opening-up, also known as the Chinese economic reform or Chinese economic miracle, refers to a variety of economic reforms in the People's Republic of China (PRC) that began in the late 20th century, after Mao Zedong's death in 1976.
cards.extra:
Deng Xiaoping's 'reform and opening' steered Mao's command economy toward markets and global trade, lifting some 800 million people out of poverty and setting China on the road to becoming the world's #2 economy and a superpower.

Region: East Asia

cards.back: The Iranian Revolution, also known as the Islamic Revolution, culminated in the overthrow of the Pahlavi dynasty in 1979.
cards.extra:
Overthrew Iran's monarchy and created the world's first modern Islamic republic under Ayatollah Khomeini — transforming Middle East politics, igniting enduring US–Iran hostility, and announcing political Islam as a global force.

Region: Middle East

cards.section: The End of the Cold War
cards.back: The Berlin Wall fell on 9 November 1989 during the Peaceful Revolution, marking the beginning of the destruction of the figurative Iron Curtain, as East Berlin transit restrictions were overwhelmed and discarded.
cards.extra:
The symbolic end of the Cold War's division of Europe. It opened the way to German reunification and the collapse of communist rule across Eastern Europe.

Region: Europe

time.title: Fall of the Berlin Wall
time.era: The End of the Cold War

cards.section: The End of the Cold War
cards.back: The Soviet Union was formally dissolved and ceased to exist as a sovereign state and subject of international law on 26 December 1991.
cards.extra:
Ended the USSR and the Cold War, creating 15 independent states (Russia, Ukraine, the Central Asian republics and more) and leaving the United States as the sole superpower.

Region: Eurasia

time.title: Dissolution of the Soviet Union
time.era: The End of the Cold War

cards.section: The End of the Cold War
cards.back: The Treaty on European Union, commonly known as the Maastricht Treaty, is the foundation treaty of the European Union (EU).
cards.extra:
Created the European Union and the path to a single currency and market — the deepest experiment in pooling national sovereignty, and a defining feature of the post-Cold-War order.

Region: Europe

time.title: Maastricht Treaty (founding the EU)
time.era: The End of the Cold War

cards.section: The End of the Cold War
cards.back: The apartheid system in South Africa was ended through a series of bilateral and multi-party negotiations between 1990 and 1993.
cards.extra:
Decades of racial segregation and white-minority rule ended with Nelson Mandela's release and the first all-race election, which made him president — a landmark for human rights and peaceful reconciliation.

Region: Southern Africa

time.title: End of Apartheid
time.era: The End of the Cold War

cards.section: The End of the Cold War
cards.back: The Rwandan genocide, also known as the Tutsi genocide, occurred from 7 April to 19 July 1994 during the Rwandan Civil War.
cards.extra:
Hutu extremists slaughtered around 800,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu in roughly 100 days while the UN and major powers failed to act — a byword for the world's failure to prevent genocide.

Region: East Africa

time.title: Rwandan genocide
time.era: The End of the Cold War

cards.section: The End of the Cold War
cards.back: The World Trade Organization (WTO) is an intergovernmental organization that regulates and facilitates international trade.
cards.extra:
Replaced the 1947 GATT to write and enforce the rules of world trade — the third pillar, with the IMF and World Bank, of the institutions that govern globalisation.

Region: Global

time.title: Founding of the WTO
time.era: The End of the Cold War

cards.back: The September 11 attacks, colloquially known as 9/11, were a coordinated series of suicide attacks perpetrated by the Islamic terrorist organization al-Qaeda against the United States in 2001.
cards.extra:
Al-Qaeda's attacks killed nearly 3,000 people and launched the US-led 'War on Terror' (Afghanistan, then Iraq), reshaping security, surveillance and geopolitics for the new century.

Region: Americas

cards.back: A major worldwide financial crisis centered in the United States took place in 2008.
cards.extra:
The collapse of the US subprime-mortgage bubble (and of Lehman Brothers) froze global finance and caused the deepest recession since the Great Depression — shaking faith in deregulated markets and reshaping economic policy worldwide.

Region: Global

cards.back: The Arab Spring was a series of pro-democracy anti-government protests, uprisings, and armed rebellions that spread across much of the Arab world in the early 2010s.
cards.extra:
Popular revolts (from late 2010) toppled long-ruling autocrats in Tunisia, Egypt, Libya and Yemen. Hopes for democracy mostly gave way to civil war (Syria, Libya) or restored authoritarianism — but it remade the modern Middle East.

Region: Middle East / N. Africa

cards.back: The global COVID-19 pandemic, caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), began with an outbreak in Wuhan, China, in December 2019.
cards.extra:
A novel coronavirus spread worldwide, killing millions and triggering unprecedented lockdowns, an economic shock and the fastest vaccine development in history — a stress test of global cooperation and of modern states.

Region: Global

cards.front: Where did the first civilisations arise — and what did the locations have in common?
cards.back: In great river valleys: Mesopotamia (Tigris–Euphrates), Egypt (the Nile), the Indus Valley, and China (the Yellow River).
cards.extra: Rivers meant irrigated surplus agriculture — and surplus meant cities, specialists, states and writing. (The Olmec show the Americas produced civilisation independently, on a different template.)

cards.front: Simultaneity — while Rome expelled its kings and Athens invented democracy (509/508 BCE), what was happening across Asia?
cards.back: Persia was the world's superpower; the Buddha was teaching in India; Confucius was teaching in China.
cards.extra: Within a couple of generations, the Mediterranean, India and China each produced ideas that still define them — see the 'Axial Age' card.

cards.back: Karl Jaspers' name for roughly 800–200 BCE, when the traditions that still organise humanity appeared almost simultaneously and independently: Greek philosophy, the Hebrew prophets, the Buddha and the Upanishads in India, Confucius and Laozi in China.
cards.extra: Why so many civilisations had their ethical breakthrough at once is one of history's great open questions — iron-age prosperity, cities and writing are the usual suspects.

cards.front: What is the structural sequence of the classical Mediterranean — Greece, Alexander, Rome?
cards.back: Greece created the culture (philosophy, drama, politics); Alexander spread it east across the Hellenistic world; Rome conquered both and built the empire that carried Greco-Roman civilisation to the whole of Europe.
cards.extra: As Horace put it: 'Captive Greece took captive her rude conqueror' — Rome won the wars, Greek culture won Rome.

cards.front: Around 1 CE, which two empires held up the two ends of Eurasia — and did they know each other?
cards.back: Rome in the west and Han China in the east, each ruling roughly a quarter of humanity. They never met — they traded silk and glass through Silk Road middlemen such as Parthian Persia.
cards.extra: The Silk Road, not diplomacy, connected the ancient superpowers. (Cross-links the Silk Road event card.)

cards.front: Trace the ancestry of the alphabet you are reading.
cards.back: Egyptian-inspired consonant signs → the Phoenician alphabet (c. 1050 BCE) → Greek, which added vowels → the Latin alphabet of Rome.
cards.extra: The same Phoenician parent also produced Hebrew, Arabic and — via Greek — Cyrillic. One Levantine trading script underlies nearly all the world's alphabets.

cards.front: Did the Roman Empire really 'fall' in 476?
cards.back: Only its western half. The eastern half — Byzantium, capital Constantinople — remained Roman in law and name for another thousand years, until the Ottomans took the city in 1453.
cards.extra: 'The fall of Rome' is a Western European story; ask a 10th-century Byzantine and the empire was doing fine.

cards.front: Which enduring faiths took shape in antiquity, and in what order?
cards.back: Judaism took its lasting, text-centred shape around the Babylonian exile (6th century BCE); Buddhism arose in India (c. 5th century BCE); Christianity in Roman Judaea (1st century CE).
cards.extra: Hinduism's Vedic roots are older still, and Islam follows in the 7th century CE — see the coming medieval era.

cards.front: While Europe passed through its 'Dark Ages' (c. 700), what was happening elsewhere?
cards.back: Tang Chang'an was the largest city on Earth; the Arab conquests were building an empire from Spain to the Indus; Baghdad was about to become the world's knowledge capital; the Maya were at their classical peak.
cards.extra: 'Dark Ages' is a label for one corner of the world — globally, the seventh and eighth centuries were bright.

cards.front: Who were the three heirs of the Roman Mediterranean?
cards.back: Byzantium (the empire's direct continuation in the east), the Islamic caliphate (which took the southern and eastern shores), and Latin Christendom (the fragmented Catholic west).
cards.extra: One Roman lake became three civilisations — and their borders are still visible in today's religions and alphabets.

cards.front: How did Greek learning get back to Europe?
cards.back: Via the Islamic world: Greek → Arabic in Abbasid Baghdad (House of Wisdom) → Latin through Spain and Sicily → the new universities → the Renaissance.
cards.extra: Europe recovered antiquity largely because Baghdad and Córdoba had kept it, corrected it, and added to it (algebra, optics, medicine).

cards.front: Why do 988 and 1054 still matter on today's map?
cards.back: The Baptism of the Rus (988) tied the East Slavs to Byzantium; the Great Schism (1054) split Catholic from Orthodox. The line between them — through the Balkans, Belarus and Ukraine — remains a live civilisational boundary.
cards.extra: The same border keeps reappearing: alphabet (Latin vs Cyrillic), church, and geopolitical orientation.

cards.front: The Mongol paradox — destroyer or connector?
cards.back: Both: the conquests killed millions and ended Baghdad's golden age, yet the Pax Mongolica made Eurasia one connected space — Marco Polo travelled it safely, and so did the Black Death.
cards.extra: Connectivity always carries both goods and shocks — the medieval version of globalisation's double edge.

cards.front: China had the ships and the head start — why did Europe take the oceans instead?
cards.back: One decision by one court ended Zheng He's fleets (1433); no European king could have ended exploration, because rivals would simply continue it. Europe's fragmentation — many competing states — was its accidental advantage.
cards.extra: Columbus was rejected by Portugal before Spain funded him: competition kept the option alive. (Cross-links the Threads great-divergence card.)

cards.front: What cluster of events around 1450–1492 'ends' the Middle Ages?
cards.back: Gutenberg's press (c. 1450), the fall of Constantinople (1453), and 1492 — Granada's fall and Columbus's landing. Information, the old eastern anchor, and the world map all broke open within one lifetime.
cards.extra: Period labels are conveniences, but this cluster is why historians draw the line here.

cards.front: Where was the centre of the medieval world economy?
cards.back: The Indian Ocean — the monsoon trade linking East Africa's Swahili coast, Arabia, India and China. Europe was a periphery buying spices and silk with silver.
cards.extra: Da Gama and Columbus were attempts to reach that system, not to build a new one — Europe's centrality came later.

cards.front: Which four empires collapsed at the end of World War I?
cards.back: The German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman and Russian empires.
cards.extra: Their breakup redrew Europe and the Middle East — and many of today's borders and conflicts trace back to it.

cards.front: Simultaneity — while Western Europe rebuilt under the US Marshall Plan (late 1940s), what was happening in Asia?
cards.back: Decolonisation and revolution: India and Pakistan won independence (1947) and the Communists won the Chinese Civil War (1949).
cards.extra: The same few post-war years remade Europe AND Asia at once — a useful corrective to a Europe-only timeline.

cards.front: What two powers and rival systems defined the Cold War (c. 1947–1991)?
cards.back: The capitalist, US-led West versus the communist, Soviet-led East.
cards.extra: Not usually direct war but proxy wars, an arms race, and a space race — see the Cuban Missile Crisis and Apollo 11.

cards.front: Name three institutions of the post-1945 world order and what each does.
cards.back: The UN (collective security), the IMF and World Bank (the global economy), and NATO (Western collective defence).
cards.extra: All three are still central today — they're covered in the World Overview deck.

cards.front: What two events, two years apart, marked the end of the Cold War?
cards.back: The fall of the Berlin Wall (1989) and the dissolution of the Soviet Union (1991).
cards.extra: Between them, communist rule collapsed across Eastern Europe and 15 new states emerged.

cards.front: What three institutions govern the global economy, and when were they born?
cards.back: The IMF and the World Bank (Bretton Woods, 1944) and the WTO (1995, succeeding the 1947 GATT).
cards.extra: Together they are the economic pillars of the post-war order — all three are in the World Overview deck.

cards.front: This deck has two Cold War 'proxy wars' — which, and what's a proxy war?
cards.back: Korea (1950–53) and Vietnam (1955–75): the superpowers fought through local wars and allies rather than each other directly.
cards.extra: Avoiding direct US–USSR combat (and nuclear war) is exactly why the Cold War stayed 'cold' between the superpowers themselves.

cards.front: How did the decolonisation of the European empires unfold after 1945?
cards.back: It rolled from Asia to Africa: India and Pakistan first (1947), then a wave across Africa peaking in the 1960 'Year of Africa', when 17 nations became independent.
cards.extra: Suez (1956) confirmed that Britain and France could no longer act as imperial great powers.

cards.back: King of Babylon who united Mesopotamia and issued one of the earliest written law codes.
cards.extra: r. c. 1792–1750 BCE. His Code — 282 laws carved in stone, the origin of 'an eye for an eye' — is a foundational moment in the history of written law and the idea that justice is set publicly by the state, not private vengeance.
cards.why: His Code — 282 laws carved in stone, the origin of 'an eye for an eye' — is a foundational moment in the history of written law and the idea that justice is set publicly by the state, not private vengeance.
cards.cue: The Babylonian king remembered for carving one of history's first law codes in stone.

cards.front: Cyrus the Great — Founder of the Persian Empire
cards.back: Founder of the Achaemenid Persian Empire, the largest the world had yet seen.
cards.extra: r. c. 559–530 BCE. He built the first true multinational empire by ruling conquered peoples with tolerance — famously freeing the Jews from Babylon — a model of imperial governance admired for millennia; his 'cylinder' is sometimes called an early charter of rights.
cards.why: He built the first true multinational empire by ruling conquered peoples with tolerance — famously freeing the Jews from Babylon — a model of imperial governance admired for millennia; his 'cylinder' is sometimes called an early charter of rights.
cards.cue: Founder of the Persian Empire, famed for freeing the Jews from Babylonian captivity.

cards.back: Emperor who unified nearly all of the Indian subcontinent under the Maurya dynasty.
cards.extra: r. c. 268–232 BCE. After a bloody conquest of Kalinga he renounced violence, embraced Buddhism and spread it across Asia — history's great example of a conqueror turning to peace, ethics and public welfare.
cards.why: After a bloody conquest of Kalinga he renounced violence, embraced Buddhism and spread it across Asia — history's great example of a conqueror turning to peace, ethics and public welfare.
cards.cue: The Indian emperor who, sickened by his own war, converted to Buddhism and spread it across Asia.

cards.front: Qin Shi Huang — First emperor of China
cards.back: The first emperor to unify China, founder of the Qin dynasty.
cards.extra: r. 221–210 BCE. He forged a single Chinese state — standardising script, weights, currency and roads, beginning the Great Wall and leaving the terracotta army — creating the centralised idea of 'China' that endures 2,000 years later.
cards.why: He forged a single Chinese state — standardising script, weights, currency and roads, beginning the Great Wall and leaving the terracotta army — creating the centralised idea of 'China' that endures 2,000 years later.
cards.cue: The first emperor of a unified China, buried with a terracotta army.

cards.front: Alexander the Great — King of Macedon, conqueror
cards.back: Macedonian king who conquered the Persian Empire and reached India, among history's greatest generals.
cards.extra: 356–323 BCE. Undefeated in battle, he spread Greek language and culture from Egypt to India ('Hellenism'), fusing East and West and shaping the classical world — all before dying at 32.
cards.why: Undefeated in battle, he spread Greek language and culture from Egypt to India ('Hellenism'), fusing East and West and shaping the classical world — all before dying at 32.
cards.cue: The undefeated Macedonian conqueror who spread Greek culture from Egypt to India by age 32.

cards.back: Roman general and statesman whose career ended the Roman Republic.
cards.extra: 100–44 BCE. His conquest of Gaul and crossing of the Rubicon made him dictator, and his assassination triggered the wars that produced the Roman Empire — his very name became a word for ruler (Kaiser, Tsar).
cards.why: His conquest of Gaul and crossing of the Rubicon made him dictator, and his assassination triggered the wars that produced the Roman Empire — his very name became a word for ruler (Kaiser, Tsar).
cards.cue: The Roman general whose crossing of the Rubicon and assassination ended the Republic.

cards.back: Rome's first emperor (Caesar's heir Octavian), founder of the Roman Empire.
cards.extra: 63 BCE–14 CE. He ended a century of civil war and began the Pax Romana — two centuries of stability that let Roman law, roads and culture spread across the Mediterranean world.
cards.why: He ended a century of civil war and began the Pax Romana — two centuries of stability that let Roman law, roads and culture spread across the Mediterranean world.
cards.cue: Caesar's heir who became Rome's first emperor and began the Pax Romana.

cards.back: The last pharaoh of Egypt, ruler of the Ptolemaic kingdom.
cards.extra: 69–30 BCE. Her alliances and romances with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony put Egypt at the centre of Rome's power struggles; her defeat and death ended both the Ptolemies and independent Egypt for two millennia.
cards.why: Her alliances and romances with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony put Egypt at the centre of Rome's power struggles; her defeat and death ended both the Ptolemies and independent Egypt for two millennia.
cards.cue: The last pharaoh of Egypt, whose alliances with Caesar and Antony entangled her in Rome's civil wars.

cards.back: Roman emperor who legalised Christianity and founded Constantinople.
cards.extra: c. 272–337 CE. By converting and backing Christianity, he turned a persecuted sect into the religion of Europe; his new capital carried the Roman legacy east for another thousand years as Byzantium.
cards.why: By converting and backing Christianity, he turned a persecuted sect into the religion of Europe; his new capital carried the Roman legacy east for another thousand years as Byzantium.
cards.cue: The Roman emperor whose conversion made Christianity the faith of Europe.

cards.front: Charlemagne — King of the Franks, Holy Roman Emperor
cards.back: King of the Franks who united much of Western Europe and was crowned emperor in 800.
cards.extra: 748–814. He reunited Western Europe for the first time since Rome, bound it to the Church and sparked a revival of learning — the founding figure of the idea of 'Europe', claimed as a father by both France and Germany.
cards.why: He reunited Western Europe for the first time since Rome, bound it to the Church and sparked a revival of learning — the founding figure of the idea of 'Europe', claimed as a father by both France and Germany.
cards.cue: The Frankish king crowned emperor in 800, called the father of Europe.

cards.front: Genghis Khan — Founder of the Mongol Empire
cards.back: Founder and khan of the Mongol Empire, the largest contiguous land empire in history.
cards.extra: c. 1162–1227. From scattered nomad tribes he built an empire from China to Europe; brutal in conquest, it also reopened the Silk Road, linking East and West in trade, technology and ideas on an unprecedented scale.
cards.why: From scattered nomad tribes he built an empire from China to Europe; brutal in conquest, it also reopened the Silk Road, linking East and West in trade, technology and ideas on an unprecedented scale.
cards.cue: The nomad who forged the largest contiguous land empire in history.

cards.front: Kublai Khan — Mongol emperor of China
cards.back: Grandson of Genghis Khan who conquered China and founded the Yuan dynasty.
cards.extra: 1215–1294. He completed the Mongol conquest of China and ruled as its emperor, opening it to the wider world — the fabled court Marco Polo described — marking the height of the Mongol Empire's reach.
cards.why: He completed the Mongol conquest of China and ruled as its emperor, opening it to the wider world — the fabled court Marco Polo described — marking the height of the Mongol Empire's reach.
cards.cue: Genghis's grandson who conquered China and hosted Marco Polo's fabled court.

cards.back: Emperor of the Mali Empire, often cited as the richest individual in history.
cards.extra: r. c. 1312–1337. His gold-laden pilgrimage to Mecca advertised Mali's fabulous wealth to the world and made Timbuktu a centre of Islamic learning — a reminder that medieval West Africa was rich, literate and globally connected.
cards.why: His gold-laden pilgrimage to Mecca advertised Mali's fabulous wealth to the world and made Timbuktu a centre of Islamic learning — a reminder that medieval West Africa was rich, literate and globally connected.
cards.cue: The Mali emperor whose gold-laden pilgrimage made him perhaps the richest person ever.

cards.back: Kurdish sultan who founded the Ayyubid dynasty and led Muslim forces in the Crusades.
cards.extra: 1137–1193. He recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187 yet won lasting fame — even among his enemies — for his chivalry and mercy, becoming the model of the noble Muslim ruler.
cards.why: He recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187 yet won lasting fame — even among his enemies — for his chivalry and mercy, becoming the model of the noble Muslim ruler.
cards.cue: The sultan who retook Jerusalem from the Crusaders and was admired even by his foes for his chivalry.

cards.back: The longest-reigning Ottoman sultan, under whom the empire reached its peak.
cards.extra: 1494–1566. He pushed the Ottoman Empire to the gates of Vienna, codified its laws (earning the name 'the Lawgiver'), and presided over a golden age of art and architecture spanning three continents.
cards.why: He pushed the Ottoman Empire to the gates of Vienna, codified its laws (earning the name 'the Lawgiver'), and presided over a golden age of art and architecture spanning three continents.
cards.cue: The sultan under whom the Ottoman Empire reached its peak, also called 'the Lawgiver'.

cards.back: The greatest Mughal emperor, who expanded and consolidated the empire across India.
cards.extra: 1542–1605. He ruled a vast, diverse India through religious tolerance and administrative genius — abolishing taxes on non-Muslims and hosting debate between faiths — a model of pluralist statecraft.
cards.why: He ruled a vast, diverse India through religious tolerance and administrative genius — abolishing taxes on non-Muslims and hosting debate between faiths — a model of pluralist statecraft.
cards.cue: The Mughal emperor who ruled a diverse India through religious tolerance and reform.

cards.back: Queen of England and Ireland, the last Tudor monarch.
cards.extra: 1533–1603. Her long reign defeated the Spanish Armada, secured Protestant England, and presided over a flowering of culture (Shakespeare) and the first English overseas ventures — the 'Elizabethan age'.
cards.why: Her long reign defeated the Spanish Armada, secured Protestant England, and presided over a flowering of culture (Shakespeare) and the first English overseas ventures — the 'Elizabethan age'.
cards.cue: The Tudor queen who defeated the Spanish Armada and presided over Shakespeare's England.

cards.front: Louis XIV — King of France, the 'Sun King'
cards.back: France's longest-reigning monarch, the model of absolute royal power.
cards.extra: 1638–1715. He concentrated all power in the crown ('L'état, c'est moi'), built Versailles, and made France the dominant power and cultural centre of Europe — the epitome of royal absolutism.
cards.why: He concentrated all power in the crown ('L'état, c'est moi'), built Versailles, and made France the dominant power and cultural centre of Europe — the epitome of royal absolutism.
cards.cue: The 'Sun King' who built Versailles and embodied royal absolutism.

cards.front: Peter the Great — Tsar of Russia
cards.back: Tsar who modernised Russia and made it a European great power.
cards.extra: 1672–1725. He dragged Russia toward the West by force — remaking the army, state and culture and building a new capital, St Petersburg — turning a backward realm into a European empire.
cards.why: He dragged Russia toward the West by force — remaking the army, state and culture and building a new capital, St Petersburg — turning a backward realm into a European empire.
cards.cue: The tsar who forcibly Westernised Russia and built a new capital as his 'window on Europe'.

cards.front: Catherine the Great — Empress of Russia
cards.back: Russia's longest-ruling female leader, who expanded the empire and its culture.
cards.extra: 1729–1796. She extended Russia's borders, corresponded with Enlightenment thinkers, and made her court a centre of European art and ideas — continuing Peter's project of a modern, imperial Russia.
cards.why: She extended Russia's borders, corresponded with Enlightenment thinkers, and made her court a centre of European art and ideas — continuing Peter's project of a modern, imperial Russia.
cards.cue: The Enlightenment-minded empress who expanded Russia and made her court a European cultural centre.

cards.back: French general who rose from the Revolution to crown himself emperor and dominate Europe.
cards.extra: 1769–1821. One of history's greatest commanders, he conquered most of Europe and — even in defeat — spread the Revolution's legacy: the Napoleonic Code, meritocracy and nationalism that reshaped the modern world.
cards.why: One of history's greatest commanders, he conquered most of Europe and — even in defeat — spread the Revolution's legacy: the Napoleonic Code, meritocracy and nationalism that reshaped the modern world.
cards.cue: The general who rose from the French Revolution to crown himself emperor and conquer Europe.

cards.back: The 'Iron Chancellor' who unified Germany under Prussian leadership.
cards.extra: 1815–1898. Through 'blood and iron' and masterful diplomacy he forged the German Empire in 1871, creating the great power whose rise would dominate European politics — and help set the stage for two world wars.
cards.why: Through 'blood and iron' and masterful diplomacy he forged the German Empire in 1871, creating the great power whose rise would dominate European politics — and help set the stage for two world wars.
cards.cue: The 'Iron Chancellor' who unified Germany through 'blood and iron'.

cards.back: President who led the United States through its Civil War.
cards.extra: 1809–1865. He preserved the Union through its bloodiest war and abolished slavery (the Emancipation Proclamation), redefining the nation around equality before his assassination — a global symbol of democratic leadership.
cards.why: He preserved the Union through its bloodiest war and abolished slavery (the Emancipation Proclamation), redefining the nation around equality before his assassination — a global symbol of democratic leadership.
cards.cue: The US president who preserved the Union and abolished slavery, then was assassinated.

cards.front: Queen Victoria — Queen of the United Kingdom
cards.back: Queen of the United Kingdom and Empress of India during Britain's imperial peak.
cards.extra: 1819–1901. Her 63-year reign named an age: the height of the British Empire — ruling a quarter of the world — and of the Industrial Revolution that reshaped how humans live.
cards.why: Her 63-year reign named an age: the height of the British Empire — ruling a quarter of the world — and of the Industrial Revolution that reshaped how humans live.
cards.cue: The queen whose long reign named the age of Britain's imperial and industrial peak.

cards.front: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk — Founder of modern Turkey
cards.back: General and first president who founded the Republic of Turkey from the fallen Ottoman Empire.
cards.extra: 1881–1938. He built a secular, modern nation-state on Ottoman ruins — remaking law, script, dress and the place of religion — the template of the modernising, Westernising strongman.
cards.why: He built a secular, modern nation-state on Ottoman ruins — remaking law, script, dress and the place of religion — the template of the modernising, Westernising strongman.
cards.cue: The general who built a secular modern republic from the ruins of the Ottoman Empire.

cards.front: George Washington — First US president & general
cards.back: Commander of the American Revolution and first president of the United States.
cards.extra: 1732–1799. He won American independence from Britain, then — crucially — gave up power voluntarily, setting the precedent of the peaceful, term-limited leader that grounds modern democracy.
cards.why: He won American independence from Britain, then — crucially — gave up power voluntarily, setting the precedent of the peaceful, term-limited leader that grounds modern democracy.
cards.cue: The general who won American independence, then set democracy's precedent by giving up power.

cards.front: Toussaint Louverture — Leader of the Haitian Revolution
cards.back: Formerly enslaved leader of the Haitian Revolution.
cards.extra: 1743–1803. He led the only successful large-scale slave revolt in history, founding Haiti — the first free Black republic — and striking a blow against slavery and colonialism that shook the whole Atlantic world.
cards.why: He led the only successful large-scale slave revolt in history, founding Haiti — the first free Black republic — and striking a blow against slavery and colonialism that shook the whole Atlantic world.
cards.cue: The former slave who led history's only successful slave revolution and founded the first Black republic.

cards.back: General who liberated much of South America from Spanish rule.
cards.extra: 1783–1830. He freed six nations — Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia and Panama — from Spain, becoming the continent's founding hero and a lasting symbol of Latin American independence and unity.
cards.why: He freed six nations — Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia and Panama — from Spain, becoming the continent's founding hero and a lasting symbol of Latin American independence and unity.
cards.cue: 'The Liberator' who freed six South American nations from Spanish rule.

cards.back: General whose campaigns unified Italy.
cards.extra: 1807–1882. His volunteer 'Redshirts' conquered the south and handed it over for unification, making him the hero of the Risorgimento — the movement that fused a patchwork of states into modern Italy.
cards.why: His volunteer 'Redshirts' conquered the south and handed it over for unification, making him the hero of the Risorgimento — the movement that fused a patchwork of states into modern Italy.
cards.cue: The red-shirted general whose volunteer army helped unify Italy.

cards.front: Sun Yat-sen — Founder of the Republic of China
cards.back: Revolutionary who overthrew China's last dynasty and founded the republic.
cards.extra: 1866–1925. He ended 2,000 years of imperial rule in 1911 and is revered by both Chinese states as 'father of the nation' — the bridge between imperial and modern China.
cards.why: He ended 2,000 years of imperial rule in 1911 and is revered by both Chinese states as 'father of the nation' — the bridge between imperial and modern China.
cards.cue: The revolutionary who toppled China's last dynasty and is honoured as 'father of the nation'.

cards.front: Vladimir Lenin — Leader of the Russian Revolution
cards.back: Bolshevik leader of the 1917 Russian Revolution, founder of the Soviet Union.
cards.extra: 1870–1924. He led the first successful communist revolution, founding the USSR and a model of one-party rule that inspired revolutions worldwide — splitting the 20th century into a capitalist–communist contest.
cards.why: He led the first successful communist revolution, founding the USSR and a model of one-party rule that inspired revolutions worldwide — splitting the 20th century into a capitalist–communist contest.
cards.cue: The Bolshevik who led the first communist revolution and founded the Soviet Union.

cards.front: Mahatma Gandhi — Leader of Indian independence
cards.back: Leader of India's independence movement, pioneer of mass nonviolent resistance.
cards.extra: 1869–1948. He won India's freedom from Britain through nonviolent civil disobedience (satyagraha), inspiring civil-rights and anti-colonial movements everywhere — from Martin Luther King to Mandela.
cards.why: He won India's freedom from Britain through nonviolent civil disobedience (satyagraha), inspiring civil-rights and anti-colonial movements everywhere — from Martin Luther King to Mandela.
cards.cue: The leader who won India's independence through mass nonviolent resistance.

cards.front: Mao Zedong — Founder of the People's Republic of China
cards.back: Communist leader who founded the People's Republic of China.
cards.extra: 1893–1976. He won the civil war and brought a fifth of humanity under communist rule, reshaping the Cold War; he unified and modernised China, but his Great Leap and Cultural Revolution killed tens of millions.
cards.why: He won the civil war and brought a fifth of humanity under communist rule, reshaping the Cold War; he unified and modernised China, but his Great Leap and Cultural Revolution killed tens of millions.
cards.cue: The communist leader who founded the People's Republic of China.

cards.front: Ho Chi Minh — Founder of communist Vietnam
cards.back: Revolutionary who led Vietnam's fight for independence and its communist north.
cards.extra: 1890–1969. He drove out French colonial rule and led the north through the war with the United States, becoming the symbol of victorious anti-colonial resistance; Vietnam reunified under his banner.
cards.why: He drove out French colonial rule and led the north through the war with the United States, becoming the symbol of victorious anti-colonial resistance; Vietnam reunified under his banner.
cards.cue: The revolutionary who led Vietnam against both French and American power.

cards.front: Kwame Nkrumah — First president of Ghana
cards.back: Leader who won Ghana's independence, the first in Sub-Saharan Africa.
cards.extra: 1909–1972. His 1957 victory opened the floodgates of African decolonisation and made him the leading voice of Pan-Africanism — the dream of a united, self-governing Africa.
cards.why: His 1957 victory opened the floodgates of African decolonisation and made him the leading voice of Pan-Africanism — the dream of a united, self-governing Africa.
cards.cue: The leader of the first Sub-Saharan colony to win independence, and champion of Pan-Africanism.

cards.front: Gamal Abdel Nasser — President of Egypt
cards.back: Egyptian president and leading figure of Arab nationalism.
cards.extra: 1918–1970. By nationalising the Suez Canal and defying the old colonial powers he became the hero of Arab nationalism and the non-aligned 'third world', reshaping Middle Eastern politics.
cards.why: By nationalising the Suez Canal and defying the old colonial powers he became the hero of Arab nationalism and the non-aligned 'third world', reshaping Middle Eastern politics.
cards.cue: The Egyptian president who nationalised the Suez Canal and led Arab nationalism.

cards.back: Revolutionary who led Cuba's communist revolution and ruled for nearly 50 years.
cards.extra: 1926–2016. He turned Cuba communist on the United States' doorstep, surviving the US embargo, the Bay of Pigs and the Cuban Missile Crisis — a lasting symbol of defiance to American power.
cards.why: He turned Cuba communist on the United States' doorstep, surviving the US embargo, the Bay of Pigs and the Cuban Missile Crisis — a lasting symbol of defiance to American power.
cards.cue: The revolutionary who brought communism to Cuba and defied the US for decades.

cards.back: Argentine doctor turned guerrilla, a leader of the Cuban Revolution.
cards.extra: 1928–1967. His role in Cuba and death fighting revolution abroad made him the global icon of rebellion — his image among the most reproduced in the world, shorthand for radical idealism.
cards.why: His role in Cuba and death fighting revolution abroad made him the global icon of rebellion — his image among the most reproduced in the world, shorthand for radical idealism.
cards.cue: The Argentine guerrilla of the Cuban Revolution whose face became the global icon of rebellion.

cards.front: Ayatollah Khomeini — Leader of the Iranian Revolution
cards.back: Cleric who led Iran's 1979 revolution and founded its Islamic Republic.
cards.extra: 1902–1989. He toppled the shah and created the world's first modern theocracy, announcing political Islam as a global force and igniting the enduring US–Iran hostility.
cards.why: He toppled the shah and created the world's first modern theocracy, announcing political Islam as a global force and igniting the enduring US–Iran hostility.
cards.cue: The cleric who overthrew Iran's shah and founded the first modern Islamic republic.

cards.front: Nelson Mandela — Anti-apartheid leader, president of South Africa
cards.back: Anti-apartheid leader who became South Africa's first Black president after 27 years in prison.
cards.extra: 1918–2013. He dismantled apartheid and, rather than seek revenge, led reconciliation between Black and white — the 20th century's great symbol of forgiveness, dignity and moral leadership.
cards.why: He dismantled apartheid and, rather than seek revenge, led reconciliation between Black and white — the 20th century's great symbol of forgiveness, dignity and moral leadership.
cards.cue: The prisoner-turned-president who ended apartheid and chose reconciliation over revenge.

cards.front: Martin Luther King Jr. — US civil-rights leader
cards.back: Baptist minister who led the American civil-rights movement.
cards.extra: 1929–1968. Through nonviolent protest ('I Have a Dream') he won landmark civil-rights and voting laws for Black Americans, becoming the moral voice of racial equality before his assassination.
cards.why: Through nonviolent protest ('I Have a Dream') he won landmark civil-rights and voting laws for Black Americans, becoming the moral voice of racial equality before his assassination.
cards.cue: The minister whose nonviolent 'I Have a Dream' movement won civil rights for Black Americans.

cards.back: Greek philosopher and mathematician, founder of a mystical school.
cards.extra: c. 570–495 BCE. He (or his school) gave the West the theorem on right triangles and the revolutionary idea that reality is fundamentally mathematical — that number underlies the cosmos — a seed of all later science.
cards.why: He (or his school) gave the West the theorem on right triangles and the revolutionary idea that reality is fundamentally mathematical — that number underlies the cosmos — a seed of all later science.
cards.cue: The Greek who taught that number underlies reality, remembered for a theorem about right triangles.

cards.back: Pre-Socratic Greek philosopher known as 'the obscure'.
cards.extra: c. 535–475 BCE. He held that everything is in constant flux ('you cannot step in the same river twice') and driven by opposites — a founding intuition of change and dialectic that echoes down to Hegel.
cards.why: He held that everything is in constant flux ('you cannot step in the same river twice') and driven by opposites — a founding intuition of change and dialectic that echoes down to Hegel.
cards.cue: The pre-Socratic who taught that all things flow — 'you cannot step in the same river twice'.

cards.back: Chinese teacher whose ethics shaped East Asian civilisation.
cards.extra: 551–479 BCE. His teachings on virtue, family, education and social harmony became the moral and political backbone of China and East Asia for two millennia — arguably history's most influential secular philosophy.
cards.why: His teachings on virtue, family, education and social harmony became the moral and political backbone of China and East Asia for two millennia — arguably history's most influential secular philosophy.
cards.cue: The Chinese sage whose ethics of virtue and social harmony shaped East Asia for millennia.

cards.back: Semi-legendary Chinese sage, traditional founder of Taoism.
cards.extra: c. 6th century BCE. The Tao Te Ching's teaching of the 'Way' — effortless action (wu wei), harmony with nature, yielding over forcing — became a pillar of Chinese thought alongside Confucianism and a global spiritual classic.
cards.why: The Tao Te Ching's teaching of the 'Way' — effortless action (wu wei), harmony with nature, yielding over forcing — became a pillar of Chinese thought alongside Confucianism and a global spiritual classic.
cards.cue: The sage of the 'Way' whose Tao Te Ching taught harmony with nature and effortless action.

cards.back: Athenian philosopher, founding figure of Western philosophy.
cards.extra: c. 470–399 BCE. He turned philosophy to how we should live and pioneered the questioning 'Socratic method'; executed for 'corrupting the youth', he became the model of the thinker who dies for truth. He wrote nothing — we know him through Plato.
cards.why: He turned philosophy to how we should live and pioneered the questioning 'Socratic method'; executed for 'corrupting the youth', he became the model of the thinker who dies for truth. He wrote nothing — we know him through Plato.
cards.cue: The Athenian who questioned everything, was executed for it, and wrote nothing himself.

cards.back: Socrates' student, founder of the Academy, a foundational Western philosopher.
