cards.extra: c. 428–348 BCE. His dialogues and Theory of Forms shaped all later philosophy (later thinkers are called 'footnotes to Plato'); 'The Republic' launched political philosophy, and his Academy was the first university.
cards.why: His dialogues and Theory of Forms shaped all later philosophy (later thinkers are called 'footnotes to Plato'); 'The Republic' launched political philosophy, and his Academy was the first university.
cards.cue: Socrates' pupil whose Theory of Forms and 'Republic' founded Western philosophy.

cards.back: Plato's student and Alexander's tutor, a founder of Western science and logic.
cards.extra: 384–322 BCE. He systematised logic, biology, ethics, politics and physics — the framework of Western and Islamic knowledge for 2,000 years — arguably the single most influential thinker in history.
cards.why: He systematised logic, biology, ethics, politics and physics — the framework of Western and Islamic knowledge for 2,000 years — arguably the single most influential thinker in history.
cards.cue: Plato's pupil and Alexander's tutor who systematised logic, science and ethics.

cards.back: Founder of Cynic philosophy, famous for ascetic provocations.
cards.extra: c. 412–323 BCE. Living in a barrel and mocking wealth and convention, he made philosophy a way of life and a challenge to social pretension — the archetype of the truth-telling outsider.
cards.why: Living in a barrel and mocking wealth and convention, he made philosophy a way of life and a challenge to social pretension — the archetype of the truth-telling outsider.
cards.cue: The philosopher who lived in a barrel and told Alexander to stop blocking his sunlight.

cards.back: Greek philosopher who founded Epicureanism.
cards.extra: 341–270 BCE. He taught that the good life is one of modest, lasting pleasure — friendship, tranquillity and freedom from fear of gods and death — a materialist ethics that shaped later humanism and the Enlightenment.
cards.why: He taught that the good life is one of modest, lasting pleasure — friendship, tranquillity and freedom from fear of gods and death — a materialist ethics that shaped later humanism and the Enlightenment.
cards.cue: The philosopher who taught that a good life means modest pleasure and freedom from fear.

cards.front: Zeno of Citium — Founder of Stoicism
cards.back: Greek philosopher who founded Stoicism.
cards.extra: c. 334–262 BCE. He launched Stoicism — that virtue is the only true good, and we should master our reactions to what we cannot control — a philosophy of resilience that guided Rome and has been rediscovered today.
cards.why: He launched Stoicism — that virtue is the only true good, and we should master our reactions to what we cannot control — a philosophy of resilience that guided Rome and has been rediscovered today.
cards.cue: The philosopher who founded Stoicism, teaching virtue and mastery over what we can't control.

cards.back: Roman Stoic philosopher, statesman and tutor to Nero.
cards.extra: c. 4 BCE–65 CE. His essays and letters made Stoic ethics practical and eloquent — on time, adversity and death — and remain among the most-read guides to living well; he was ordered to kill himself by his former pupil Nero.
cards.why: His essays and letters made Stoic ethics practical and eloquent — on time, adversity and death — and remain among the most-read guides to living well; he was ordered to kill himself by his former pupil Nero.
cards.cue: The Roman Stoic and Nero's tutor whose letters remain a classic guide to living well.

cards.back: Former slave who became an influential Stoic teacher.
cards.extra: c. 50–135 CE. Born a slave, he taught that freedom lies in controlling one's own judgements, not one's circumstances — the purest statement of Stoic inner freedom, a direct influence on Marcus Aurelius and modern therapy.
cards.why: Born a slave, he taught that freedom lies in controlling one's own judgements, not one's circumstances — the purest statement of Stoic inner freedom, a direct influence on Marcus Aurelius and modern therapy.
cards.cue: The former slave whose Stoic teaching located true freedom in the control of one's own mind.

cards.back: Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher, last of the 'Five Good Emperors'.
cards.extra: 121–180 CE. The rare 'philosopher-king', his private 'Meditations' — Stoic reflections written on military campaign — is one of history's most beloved books on duty, mortality and self-mastery.
cards.why: The rare 'philosopher-king', his private 'Meditations' — Stoic reflections written on military campaign — is one of history's most beloved books on duty, mortality and self-mastery.
cards.cue: The Roman emperor whose private 'Meditations' became a timeless Stoic classic.

cards.front: Augustine of Hippo — Christian philosopher & theologian
cards.back: Bishop and philosopher, the most influential of the Church Fathers.
cards.extra: 354–430. His 'Confessions' and 'City of God' fused Christianity with Greek philosophy, shaping Western theology, the idea of the inner self, and doctrines from original sin to just war for over a millennium.
cards.why: His 'Confessions' and 'City of God' fused Christianity with Greek philosophy, shaping Western theology, the idea of the inner self, and doctrines from original sin to just war for over a millennium.
cards.cue: The North African bishop whose 'Confessions' fused Christianity with Greek philosophy.

cards.back: Persian polymath, among the greatest thinkers of the Islamic Golden Age.
cards.extra: 980–1037. His 'Canon of Medicine' was the standard medical text in Europe and the Islamic world for 600 years, and his philosophy preserved and advanced Aristotle — a vital bridge of knowledge from antiquity to Europe.
cards.why: His 'Canon of Medicine' was the standard medical text in Europe and the Islamic world for 600 years, and his philosophy preserved and advanced Aristotle — a vital bridge of knowledge from antiquity to Europe.
cards.cue: The Persian polymath whose 'Canon of Medicine' was Europe's standard text for 600 years.

cards.back: Persian theologian and mystic, a towering figure of Islamic thought.
cards.extra: 1058–1111. He reconciled reason with Sufi mysticism and critiqued the philosophers, decisively shaping the direction of mainstream Sunni Islam for centuries after.
cards.why: He reconciled reason with Sufi mysticism and critiqued the philosophers, decisively shaping the direction of mainstream Sunni Islam for centuries after.
cards.cue: The theologian whose critique of the philosophers reshaped mainstream Sunni Islam.

cards.back: Philosopher and jurist of Muslim Spain, the great commentator on Aristotle.
cards.extra: 1126–1198. His commentaries reintroduced Aristotle to Christian Europe, igniting medieval philosophy and the reason-versus-faith debate; to the West he was simply 'the Commentator'.
cards.why: His commentaries reintroduced Aristotle to Christian Europe, igniting medieval philosophy and the reason-versus-faith debate; to the West he was simply 'the Commentator'.
cards.cue: The philosopher of Muslim Spain whose Aristotle commentaries reawakened European thought.

cards.back: Jewish philosopher, jurist and physician of the medieval Islamic world.
cards.extra: 1138–1204. His 'Guide for the Perplexed' reconciled Jewish scripture with Aristotelian reason, making him the foremost Jewish philosopher and a bridge between Islamic, Jewish and Christian thought.
cards.why: His 'Guide for the Perplexed' reconciled Jewish scripture with Aristotelian reason, making him the foremost Jewish philosopher and a bridge between Islamic, Jewish and Christian thought.
cards.cue: The medieval Jewish sage whose 'Guide for the Perplexed' reconciled faith and reason.

cards.back: Medieval Italian theologian, the central philosopher of the Catholic Church.
cards.extra: 1225–1274. His 'Summa Theologica' synthesised Aristotle with Christianity, becoming the backbone of Catholic theology and a monument of systematic reason applied to faith.
cards.why: His 'Summa Theologica' synthesised Aristotle with Christianity, becoming the backbone of Catholic theology and a monument of systematic reason applied to faith.
cards.cue: The medieval friar whose 'Summa' fused Aristotle with Christian theology.

cards.back: The leading scholar of the Northern Renaissance, a Christian humanist.
cards.extra: 1466–1536. His new Greek New Testament and satire ('In Praise of Folly') urged reform and reason within the Church, preparing the ground for the Reformation while pleading for tolerance — 'the prince of the humanists'.
cards.why: His new Greek New Testament and satire ('In Praise of Folly') urged reform and reason within the Church, preparing the ground for the Reformation while pleading for tolerance — 'the prince of the humanists'.
cards.cue: The 'prince of the humanists' whose scholarship and satire prepared the way for the Reformation.

cards.back: Florentine writer, founder of modern political science.
cards.extra: 1469–1527. 'The Prince' broke from moral idealism to analyse power as it really is — cold, pragmatic, sometimes ruthless — founding realist political thought and giving us the word 'Machiavellian'.
cards.why: 'The Prince' broke from moral idealism to analyse power as it really is — cold, pragmatic, sometimes ruthless — founding realist political thought and giving us the word 'Machiavellian'.
cards.cue: The Florentine whose 'The Prince' analysed power stripped of moral pretence.

cards.front: Michel de Montaigne — French essayist & philosopher
cards.back: French thinker who invented the personal essay.
cards.extra: 1533–1592. By turning honest, sceptical attention on himself ('What do I know?'), he created the essay as a form and a model of humane, self-examining thought that shaped modern individualism.
cards.why: By turning honest, sceptical attention on himself ('What do I know?'), he created the essay as a form and a model of humane, self-examining thought that shaped modern individualism.
cards.cue: The French thinker who invented the essay by turning sceptical attention on himself.

cards.back: English philosopher and statesman, a father of the scientific method.
cards.extra: 1561–1626. He championed knowledge built from observation and experiment rather than authority — laying the philosophical foundation of modern science and its promise to master nature ('knowledge is power').
cards.why: He championed knowledge built from observation and experiment rather than authority — laying the philosophical foundation of modern science and its promise to master nature ('knowledge is power').
cards.cue: The philosopher who founded the experimental scientific method — 'knowledge is power'.

cards.back: English political philosopher, author of 'Leviathan'.
cards.extra: 1588–1679. He argued that without strong authority life is 'nasty, brutish and short', so people surrender freedom to a sovereign for security — founding the modern idea of the social contract.
cards.why: He argued that without strong authority life is 'nasty, brutish and short', so people surrender freedom to a sovereign for security — founding the modern idea of the social contract.
cards.cue: The philosopher who argued life without the state is 'nasty, brutish and short'.

cards.back: French philosopher and mathematician, the father of modern philosophy.
cards.extra: 1596–1650. By doubting everything until he reached 'I think, therefore I am', he made the reasoning mind the foundation of knowledge; he also invented analytic geometry, uniting algebra and geometry.
cards.why: By doubting everything until he reached 'I think, therefore I am', he made the reasoning mind the foundation of knowledge; he also invented analytic geometry, uniting algebra and geometry.
cards.cue: The philosopher who doubted everything down to 'I think, therefore I am'.

cards.back: Dutch rationalist philosopher, a founder of modern secular thought.
cards.extra: 1632–1677. He identified God with nature itself and argued for reason, tolerance and free thought — radical enough to be excommunicated — becoming a hero of the Enlightenment and secularism.
cards.why: He identified God with nature itself and argued for reason, tolerance and free thought — radical enough to be excommunicated — becoming a hero of the Enlightenment and secularism.
cards.cue: The excommunicated philosopher who equated God with nature and championed free thought.

cards.back: English philosopher, a father of liberalism and empiricism.
cards.extra: 1632–1704. He argued the mind begins as a 'blank slate' and that people have natural rights to life, liberty and property — ideas that shaped the Enlightenment, the American Revolution and modern liberal democracy.
cards.why: He argued the mind begins as a 'blank slate' and that people have natural rights to life, liberty and property — ideas that shaped the Enlightenment, the American Revolution and modern liberal democracy.
cards.cue: The philosopher of natural rights and the 'blank slate' who fathered liberalism.

cards.back: German philosopher and mathematician, co-inventor of calculus.
cards.extra: 1646–1716. He invented calculus independently of Newton (our notation is his), devised binary arithmetic and calculating machines that prefigured computing, and ranged across law, science and philosophy as a last universal genius.
cards.why: He invented calculus independently of Newton (our notation is his), devised binary arithmetic and calculating machines that prefigured computing, and ranged across law, science and philosophy as a last universal genius.
cards.cue: The German polymath who co-invented calculus and devised the binary system.

cards.back: French Enlightenment thinker, author of 'The Spirit of the Laws'.
cards.extra: 1689–1755. He argued for the separation of powers — dividing government into legislative, executive and judicial branches — the design written directly into the US Constitution and most modern democracies.
cards.why: He argued for the separation of powers — dividing government into legislative, executive and judicial branches — the design written directly into the US Constitution and most modern democracies.
cards.cue: The Enlightenment thinker whose 'separation of powers' shaped the US Constitution.

cards.back: French writer and philosopher, the leading voice of the Enlightenment.
cards.extra: 1694–1778. A brilliant satirist and campaigner against tyranny, superstition and intolerance, he became the very symbol of free speech and reason — and of the Enlightenment's war on dogma.
cards.why: A brilliant satirist and campaigner against tyranny, superstition and intolerance, he became the very symbol of free speech and reason — and of the Enlightenment's war on dogma.
cards.cue: The witty French campaigner against tyranny and intolerance who symbolises the Enlightenment.

cards.back: Scottish philosopher, the greatest of the British empiricists.
cards.extra: 1711–1776. His radical scepticism about causation, the self and religion pushed empiricism to its limit — 'awakening Kant from his dogmatic slumber' — and remains a benchmark of rigorous, naturalistic philosophy.
cards.why: His radical scepticism about causation, the self and religion pushed empiricism to its limit — 'awakening Kant from his dogmatic slumber' — and remains a benchmark of rigorous, naturalistic philosophy.
cards.cue: The Scottish sceptic whose empiricism 'awoke Kant from his dogmatic slumber'.

cards.back: Enlightenment philosopher of politics, education and human nature.
cards.extra: 1712–1778. His 'Social Contract' argued government must rest on the people's 'general will' — inspiring the French Revolution and modern democracy — while his ideas on nature and feeling launched Romanticism ('man is born free, but everywhere is in chains').
cards.why: His 'Social Contract' argued government must rest on the people's 'general will' — inspiring the French Revolution and modern democracy — while his ideas on nature and feeling launched Romanticism ('man is born free, but everywhere is in chains').
cards.cue: The philosopher of the 'general will' — 'man is born free, but everywhere is in chains'.

cards.back: German philosopher, the central figure of modern philosophy.
cards.extra: 1724–1804. His 'Critique of Pure Reason' reconciled reason and experience and reset the terms of philosophy; his ethics — act only on rules you could will to be universal (the categorical imperative) — anchors modern moral thought. Nearly all later philosophy responds to him.
cards.why: His 'Critique of Pure Reason' reconciled reason and experience and reset the terms of philosophy; his ethics — act only on rules you could will to be universal (the categorical imperative) — anchors modern moral thought. Nearly all later philosophy responds to him.
cards.cue: The philosopher of the 'categorical imperative' who reset the terms of modern thought.

cards.back: Scottish moral philosopher, the father of modern economics.
cards.extra: 1723–1790. 'The Wealth of Nations' explained how self-interest, guided by the 'invisible hand' of markets, can create broad prosperity — founding economics and the intellectual case for free markets.
cards.why: 'The Wealth of Nations' explained how self-interest, guided by the 'invisible hand' of markets, can create broad prosperity — founding economics and the intellectual case for free markets.
cards.cue: The philosopher whose 'invisible hand' founded modern economics.

cards.front: G. W. F. Hegel — German idealist philosopher
cards.back: German philosopher of history and the dialectic.
cards.extra: 1770–1831. He saw history as the unfolding of reason and freedom through the clash of ideas (the 'dialectic') — a framework that shaped modern thought and, turned upside down, gave Marx his theory of history.
cards.why: He saw history as the unfolding of reason and freedom through the clash of ideas (the 'dialectic') — a framework that shaped modern thought and, turned upside down, gave Marx his theory of history.
cards.cue: The philosopher who saw history as reason unfolding through dialectical conflict.

cards.back: German philosopher of pessimism, author of 'The World as Will and Representation'.
cards.extra: 1788–1860. He argued that a blind, striving 'Will' drives all life and dooms us to suffering — bringing Eastern thought into Western philosophy and deeply shaping Nietzsche, Freud and Wagner.
cards.why: He argued that a blind, striving 'Will' drives all life and dooms us to suffering — bringing Eastern thought into Western philosophy and deeply shaping Nietzsche, Freud and Wagner.
cards.cue: The great pessimist who saw a blind striving 'Will' behind all suffering.

cards.back: Danish philosopher and theologian, the father of existentialism.
cards.extra: 1813–1855. He put individual existence, anxiety, faith and the 'leap' of subjective choice at the centre of philosophy — founding existentialism and reframing what it means to live authentically.
cards.why: He put individual existence, anxiety, faith and the 'leap' of subjective choice at the centre of philosophy — founding existentialism and reframing what it means to live authentically.
cards.cue: The melancholy Dane who fathered existentialism with the 'leap of faith'.

cards.back: Philosopher and economist whose ideas founded communism.
cards.extra: 1818–1883. He argued history is driven by class struggle and that capitalism would give way to socialism; 'The Communist Manifesto' and 'Capital' inspired the revolutions, states and movements that shaped the whole 20th century.
cards.why: He argued history is driven by class struggle and that capitalism would give way to socialism; 'The Communist Manifesto' and 'Capital' inspired the revolutions, states and movements that shaped the whole 20th century.
cards.cue: The philosopher of class struggle whose ideas founded communism.

cards.back: English philosopher and economist, the leading liberal thinker of his age.
cards.extra: 1806–1873. 'On Liberty' made the classic case for individual freedom and free speech (the 'harm principle'), and he advanced utilitarian ethics and early feminism — a cornerstone of modern liberalism.
cards.why: 'On Liberty' made the classic case for individual freedom and free speech (the 'harm principle'), and he advanced utilitarian ethics and early feminism — a cornerstone of modern liberalism.
cards.cue: The philosopher of individual liberty and free speech — the 'harm principle'.

cards.back: German philosopher who declared 'God is dead' and challenged Western morality.
cards.extra: 1844–1900. He diagnosed the collapse of religious meaning and called for creating new values ('the will to power', the 'Übermensch'), profoundly shaping existentialism, psychology, art and modern thought.
cards.why: He diagnosed the collapse of religious meaning and called for creating new values ('the will to power', the 'Übermensch'), profoundly shaping existentialism, psychology, art and modern thought.
cards.cue: The philosopher who declared 'God is dead' and demanded we create new values.

cards.back: German thinker, author of 'The Decline of the West'.
cards.extra: 1880–1936. He argued that civilisations are living organisms that rise, mature and inevitably decay — a sweeping, controversial philosophy of history that popularised the idea of cyclical civilisational decline.
cards.why: He argued that civilisations are living organisms that rise, mature and inevitably decay — a sweeping, controversial philosophy of history that popularised the idea of cyclical civilisational decline.
cards.cue: The thinker whose 'Decline of the West' cast civilisations as organisms that must rise and fall.

cards.back: Austrian neurologist, founder of psychoanalysis.
cards.extra: 1856–1939. He proposed the unconscious mind, repression, and the role of childhood and sexuality — transforming psychology and psychiatry and reshaping how the modern world understands the self, dreams and desire.
cards.why: He proposed the unconscious mind, repression, and the role of childhood and sexuality — transforming psychology and psychiatry and reshaping how the modern world understands the self, dreams and desire.
cards.cue: The founder of psychoanalysis who put the unconscious mind at the centre of human behaviour.

cards.front: Ludwig Wittgenstein — Philosopher of language & logic
cards.back: One of the 20th century's most important philosophers, of language and logic.
cards.extra: 1889–1951. He argued that many philosophical problems are confusions of language — 'the limits of my language are the limits of my world' — twice revolutionising philosophy and founding much of analytic thought.
cards.why: He argued that many philosophical problems are confusions of language — 'the limits of my language are the limits of my world' — twice revolutionising philosophy and founding much of analytic thought.
cards.cue: The philosopher who argued that philosophy's problems are really puzzles of language.

cards.back: French philosopher and writer, the face of existentialism.
cards.extra: 1905–1980. 'Existence precedes essence': we are 'condemned to be free' and wholly responsible for what we make of ourselves — the definitive statement of existentialism, which dominated post-war thought.
cards.why: 'Existence precedes essence': we are 'condemned to be free' and wholly responsible for what we make of ourselves — the definitive statement of existentialism, which dominated post-war thought.
cards.cue: The existentialist who said we are 'condemned to be free' — existence precedes essence.

cards.back: German-American political theorist of power, freedom and totalitarianism.
cards.extra: 1906–1975. She analysed the roots of totalitarianism and coined 'the banality of evil' to describe how ordinary people enable atrocities — among the most penetrating political thinkers on the 20th century's horrors.
cards.why: She analysed the roots of totalitarianism and coined 'the banality of evil' to describe how ordinary people enable atrocities — among the most penetrating political thinkers on the 20th century's horrors.
cards.cue: The theorist of totalitarianism who coined 'the banality of evil'.

cards.back: Greek mathematician, 'the father of geometry'.
cards.extra: c. 300 BCE. His 'Elements' built all of geometry from a handful of axioms by pure logical proof — the model of rigorous reasoning taught for over 2,000 years and the template for mathematics itself.
cards.why: His 'Elements' built all of geometry from a handful of axioms by pure logical proof — the model of rigorous reasoning taught for over 2,000 years and the template for mathematics itself.
cards.cue: The Greek whose 'Elements' built geometry from axioms and defined mathematical proof.

cards.back: Greek mathematician, physicist and engineer, antiquity's greatest scientist.
cards.extra: c. 287–212 BCE. He founded the study of levers and buoyancy ('Eureka!'), approximated pi, and anticipated integral calculus — uniting pure mathematics with ingenious machines like no one before him.
cards.why: He founded the study of levers and buoyancy ('Eureka!'), approximated pi, and anticipated integral calculus — uniting pure mathematics with ingenious machines like no one before him.
cards.cue: The Greek who cried 'Eureka!', founded the study of levers and buoyancy, and nearly invented calculus.

cards.back: Arab scientist, a pioneer of optics and the experimental method.
cards.extra: c. 965–1040. He explained how vision actually works and insisted that theories be tested by experiment — an early, explicit statement of the scientific method, centuries before Europe's.
cards.why: He explained how vision actually works and insisted that theories be tested by experiment — an early, explicit statement of the scientific method, centuries before Europe's.
cards.cue: The Arab scientist who founded optics and pioneered the experimental scientific method.

cards.back: Polish astronomer who placed the Sun, not the Earth, at the centre.
cards.extra: 1473–1543. His heliocentric model demoted the Earth from the centre of the universe — launching the Scientific Revolution and forcing humanity to rethink its place in the cosmos.
cards.why: His heliocentric model demoted the Earth from the centre of the universe — launching the Scientific Revolution and forcing humanity to rethink its place in the cosmos.
cards.cue: The astronomer who moved the Earth from the centre of the universe and put the Sun there.

cards.back: German astronomer who found the laws of planetary motion.
cards.extra: 1571–1630. He discovered that planets move in ellipses, not circles — turning Copernicus's idea into precise mathematical law and paving the way for Newton's gravity.
cards.why: He discovered that planets move in ellipses, not circles — turning Copernicus's idea into precise mathematical law and paving the way for Newton's gravity.
cards.cue: The astronomer who discovered that planets orbit in ellipses, giving Newton his footing.

cards.back: Italian scientist, 'the father of modern science'.
cards.extra: 1564–1642. He turned the telescope on the heavens (Jupiter's moons, Venus's phases), founded experimental physics, and defended Copernicus — tried by the Inquisition, he became the symbol of science versus dogma.
cards.why: He turned the telescope on the heavens (Jupiter's moons, Venus's phases), founded experimental physics, and defended Copernicus — tried by the Inquisition, he became the symbol of science versus dogma.
cards.cue: The scientist whose telescope backed Copernicus and got him tried by the Inquisition.

cards.back: English physicist and mathematician, one of the greatest scientists ever.
cards.extra: 1643–1727. His laws of motion and universal gravitation explained falling apples and orbiting planets with one mathematics, and he co-invented calculus — completing the Scientific Revolution and defining physics for 200 years.
cards.why: His laws of motion and universal gravitation explained falling apples and orbiting planets with one mathematics, and he co-invented calculus — completing the Scientific Revolution and defining physics for 200 years.
cards.cue: The scientist whose laws of motion and gravity, plus calculus, completed the Scientific Revolution.

cards.back: French chemist, 'the father of modern chemistry'.
cards.extra: 1743–1794. He named oxygen, showed that matter is conserved in chemical reactions, and built chemistry's modern system of elements — before being guillotined in the French Revolution.
cards.why: He named oxygen, showed that matter is conserved in chemical reactions, and built chemistry's modern system of elements — before being guillotined in the French Revolution.
cards.cue: The 'father of modern chemistry' who named oxygen and was guillotined in the Revolution.

cards.back: English scientist who discovered electromagnetic induction.
cards.extra: 1791–1867. A self-taught genius, he showed that magnetism can generate electricity — the principle behind every generator and electric motor — and gave physics the idea of the 'field'.
cards.why: A self-taught genius, he showed that magnetism can generate electricity — the principle behind every generator and electric motor — and gave physics the idea of the 'field'.
cards.cue: The self-taught scientist whose discovery of induction underlies every motor and generator.

cards.back: English naturalist who discovered evolution by natural selection.
cards.extra: 1809–1882. 'On the Origin of Species' showed that all life evolved from common ancestors through natural selection — the unifying theory of biology and one of the most consequential ideas in history.
cards.why: 'On the Origin of Species' showed that all life evolved from common ancestors through natural selection — the unifying theory of biology and one of the most consequential ideas in history.
cards.cue: The naturalist whose theory of evolution by natural selection unified all of biology.

cards.back: French scientist who founded microbiology and germ theory.
cards.extra: 1822–1895. He proved that germs cause disease, and invented pasteurisation and new vaccines — transforming medicine, food safety and public health and saving countless lives.
cards.why: He proved that germs cause disease, and invented pasteurisation and new vaccines — transforming medicine, food safety and public health and saving countless lives.
cards.cue: The scientist who proved that germs cause disease and pioneered vaccination and food sterilisation.

cards.back: Austrian friar who discovered the laws of heredity.
cards.extra: 1822–1884. His pea-plant experiments revealed how traits pass through discrete units — genes — founding genetics, though his work was ignored until decades after his death.
cards.why: His pea-plant experiments revealed how traits pass through discrete units — genes — founding genetics, though his work was ignored until decades after his death.
cards.cue: The friar whose pea-plant experiments founded the science of genetics.

cards.back: Scottish physicist who unified electricity, magnetism and light.
cards.extra: 1831–1879. His equations showed that light is an electromagnetic wave and united electricity and magnetism — the greatest leap in physics between Newton and Einstein, and the basis of all radio and wireless.
cards.why: His equations showed that light is an electromagnetic wave and united electricity and magnetism — the greatest leap in physics between Newton and Einstein, and the basis of all radio and wireless.
cards.cue: The physicist whose equations unified electricity, magnetism and light.

cards.back: Pioneer of radioactivity, the first person to win two Nobel Prizes.
cards.extra: 1867–1934. She discovered radioactivity and the elements polonium and radium, founded nuclear science and cancer radiotherapy, and — the first woman to win a Nobel, and the first person to win two — became the icon of women in science.
cards.why: She discovered radioactivity and the elements polonium and radium, founded nuclear science and cancer radiotherapy, and — the first woman to win a Nobel, and the first person to win two — became the icon of women in science.
cards.cue: The pioneer of radioactivity and only person to win Nobels in two different sciences.

cards.back: German-born physicist, the most famous scientist of the modern age.
cards.extra: 1879–1955. His theories of relativity remade our understanding of space, time, gravity and energy (E=mc²), opening the nuclear age and modern cosmology — and making him the very image of genius.
cards.why: His theories of relativity remade our understanding of space, time, gravity and energy (E=mc²), opening the nuclear age and modern cosmology — and making him the very image of genius.
cards.cue: The physicist whose relativity remade space and time and gave us E=mc².

cards.back: Danish physicist, a founder of quantum theory.
cards.extra: 1885–1962. He built the first quantum model of the atom and shaped quantum mechanics — the strange physics of the very small that underlies all modern electronics — and mentored a generation of physicists.
cards.why: He built the first quantum model of the atom and shaped quantum mechanics — the strange physics of the very small that underlies all modern electronics — and mentored a generation of physicists.
cards.cue: The Danish founder of quantum theory who built the first quantum model of the atom.

cards.back: German physicist, a founder of quantum mechanics.
cards.extra: 1901–1976. His uncertainty principle — that a particle's exact position and momentum cannot both be known — is a pillar of quantum mechanics and a profound limit on what can be known about nature.
cards.why: His uncertainty principle — that a particle's exact position and momentum cannot both be known — is a pillar of quantum mechanics and a profound limit on what can be known about nature.
cards.cue: The physicist whose 'uncertainty principle' set a fundamental limit on what we can know.

cards.back: Serbian-American inventor who pioneered alternating-current electricity.
cards.extra: 1856–1943. His AC system won the 'war of the currents' and powers the modern electrical grid; his work on motors and wireless made him the archetype of the visionary inventor.
cards.why: His AC system won the 'war of the currents' and powers the modern electrical grid; his work on motors and wireless made him the archetype of the visionary inventor.
cards.cue: The inventor whose alternating-current system powers the world's electrical grids.

cards.back: American inventor of the practical light bulb, phonograph and much else.
cards.extra: 1847–1931. With the phonograph, a workable light bulb and the first industrial research lab, he turned invention into an industry — and built the first electric power systems that lit the modern world.
cards.why: With the phonograph, a workable light bulb and the first industrial research lab, he turned invention into an industry — and built the first electric power systems that lit the modern world.
cards.cue: The inventor of the phonograph and practical light bulb who industrialised invention itself.

cards.back: British mathematician, the father of computer science and AI.
cards.extra: 1912–1954. He defined the idea of the computer (the 'Turing machine'), broke Nazi Germany's Enigma code (shortening WWII), and posed the founding question of artificial intelligence — before being persecuted for being gay.
cards.why: He defined the idea of the computer (the 'Turing machine'), broke Nazi Germany's Enigma code (shortening WWII), and posed the founding question of artificial intelligence — before being persecuted for being gay.
cards.cue: The mathematician who founded computer science, broke the Enigma code, and framed the AI question.

cards.front: Watson, Crick & Franklin — Discoverers of DNA's structure
cards.back: The scientists who discovered the double-helix structure of DNA.
cards.extra: 1950s. Their 1953 model of DNA revealed how life stores and copies its genetic code — launching molecular biology, genetics and biotechnology; Rosalind Franklin's X-ray images were crucial and long under-credited.
cards.why: Their 1953 model of DNA revealed how life stores and copies its genetic code — launching molecular biology, genetics and biotechnology; Rosalind Franklin's X-ray images were crucial and long under-credited.
cards.cue: The team who cracked the double-helix structure of DNA in 1953.

cards.back: American agronomist, father of the 'Green Revolution'.
cards.extra: 1914–2009. His high-yield wheat and farming methods hugely raised food production in the developing world — credited with saving perhaps a billion lives from famine, one of history's greatest humanitarian feats.
cards.why: His high-yield wheat and farming methods hugely raised food production in the developing world — credited with saving perhaps a billion lives from famine, one of history's greatest humanitarian feats.
cards.cue: The agronomist whose 'Green Revolution' crops are credited with saving a billion from famine.

cards.back: British computer scientist, inventor of the World Wide Web.
cards.extra: b. 1955. In 1989 he invented the Web — URLs, HTTP and HTML — and gave it away free, creating the platform that reshaped information, commerce and daily life for billions.
cards.why: In 1989 he invented the Web — URLs, HTTP and HTML — and gave it away free, creating the platform that reshaped information, commerce and daily life for billions.
cards.cue: The scientist who invented the World Wide Web in 1989 — and gave it away for free.

cards.back: The founding prophet of Judaism, who led the Hebrews out of Egypt.
cards.extra: c. 13th century BCE. The central figure of the Torah, he is the archetypal lawgiver — receiving the Ten Commandments — and a foundational figure for Judaism, Christianity and Islam alike.
cards.why: The central figure of the Torah, he is the archetypal lawgiver — receiving the Ten Commandments — and a foundational figure for Judaism, Christianity and Islam alike.
cards.cue: The prophet who led the Hebrews out of Egypt and received the Ten Commandments.

cards.back: The Indian prince whose enlightenment founded Buddhism.
cards.extra: c. 5th century BCE. His teaching that suffering springs from desire, and can be ended through the Middle Way, grew into one of the world's great religions and shaped the philosophy and culture of much of Asia.
cards.why: His teaching that suffering springs from desire, and can be ended through the Middle Way, grew into one of the world's great religions and shaped the philosophy and culture of much of Asia.
cards.cue: The prince who, seeking the end of suffering, achieved enlightenment and founded a world religion.

cards.front: Jesus of Nazareth — Central figure of Christianity
cards.back: Jewish preacher whose life and teachings founded Christianity.
cards.extra: c. 4 BCE–30 CE. Worshipped by Christians as the Son of God, his message of love, forgiveness and salvation became the world's largest religion and a bedrock of Western civilisation; the calendar itself counts from his birth.
cards.why: Worshipped by Christians as the Son of God, his message of love, forgiveness and salvation became the world's largest religion and a bedrock of Western civilisation; the calendar itself counts from his birth.
cards.cue: The Jewish preacher whose life and teachings founded the world's largest religion.

cards.back: The Arab prophet who founded Islam.
cards.extra: c. 570–632. Muslims regard him as God's final prophet; his revelations (the Quran) and community united Arabia and launched a faith and civilisation that spread from Spain to Indonesia — now nearly a quarter of humanity.
cards.why: Muslims regard him as God's final prophet; his revelations (the Quran) and community united Arabia and launched a faith and civilisation that spread from Spain to Indonesia — now nearly a quarter of humanity.
cards.cue: The Arab prophet whose revelations founded Islam and united Arabia.

cards.back: German monk who launched the Protestant Reformation.
cards.extra: 1483–1546. His '95 Theses' (1517) challenged the Catholic Church, split Western Christianity, and — by insisting on scripture and individual conscience — helped birth the modern individual (and, through his Bible, the German language).
cards.why: His '95 Theses' (1517) challenged the Catholic Church, split Western Christianity, and — by insisting on scripture and individual conscience — helped birth the modern individual (and, through his Bible, the German language).
cards.cue: The monk whose 95 Theses split Western Christianity and launched the Reformation.

cards.back: French reformer whose theology shaped much of Protestantism.
cards.extra: 1509–1564. His rigorous system (predestination, discipline, diligence) built the Reformed and Presbyterian churches and, some argue, the 'Protestant work ethic' that shaped modern capitalism and the English-speaking world.
cards.why: His rigorous system (predestination, discipline, diligence) built the Reformed and Presbyterian churches and, some argue, the 'Protestant work ethic' that shaped modern capitalism and the English-speaking world.
cards.cue: The stern reformer of Geneva whose theology shaped Protestantism and the 'work ethic'.

cards.back: Indian spiritual teacher, founder of Sikhism.
cards.extra: 1469–1539. He founded Sikhism on the oneness of God, the equality of all people (rejecting caste) and honest work — now the world's fifth-largest religion, centred in the Punjab.
cards.why: He founded Sikhism on the oneness of God, the equality of all people (rejecting caste) and honest work — now the world's fifth-largest religion, centred in the Punjab.
cards.cue: The teacher who founded Sikhism on one God and the equality of all people.

cards.back: Albanian-Indian nun who served the poorest of the poor in Calcutta.
cards.extra: 1910–1997. Her decades caring for the dying and destitute made her the 20th century's global symbol of compassion and charity, and a canonised saint — though not without controversy.
cards.why: Her decades caring for the dying and destitute made her the 20th century's global symbol of compassion and charity, and a canonised saint — though not without controversy.
cards.cue: The nun whose work among Calcutta's dying made her the modern symbol of charity.

cards.front: The 14th Dalai Lama — Tibetan Buddhist leader
cards.back: The spiritual leader of Tibetan Buddhism, in exile since 1959.
cards.extra: b. 1935. Forced into exile by China's takeover of Tibet, he became a global voice for nonviolence, compassion and dialogue between science and spirituality — and the face of the Tibetan cause.
cards.why: Forced into exile by China's takeover of Tibet, he became a global voice for nonviolence, compassion and dialogue between science and spirituality — and the face of the Tibetan cause.
cards.cue: The exiled Tibetan monk who became a global voice for nonviolence and compassion.

cards.back: Florentine painter who broke medieval flatness and pioneered realism.
cards.extra: c. 1267–1337. He pulled painting toward lifelike depth, emotion and three-dimensional space — the crucial first step from flat medieval icons toward the Renaissance.
cards.why: He pulled painting toward lifelike depth, emotion and three-dimensional space — the crucial first step from flat medieval icons toward the Renaissance.
cards.cue: The pre-Renaissance painter whose lifelike depth and emotion broke medieval flatness.

cards.back: Florentine architect who built the dome of Florence Cathedral and discovered linear perspective.
cards.extra: 1377–1446. He raised the largest dome since antiquity — without scaffolding — and rediscovered linear perspective, the mathematical technique that let artists paint convincing depth, launching Renaissance art and architecture.
cards.why: He raised the largest dome since antiquity — without scaffolding — and rediscovered linear perspective, the mathematical technique that let artists paint convincing depth, launching Renaissance art and architecture.
cards.cue: The architect who raised Florence's giant dome and discovered linear perspective.

cards.back: Florentine sculptor who revived lifelike, classical statuary.
cards.extra: c. 1386–1466. His expressive, anatomically real sculptures — a bronze 'David', the first free-standing nude since antiquity — set the standard for Renaissance sculpture and paved the way for Michelangelo.
cards.why: His expressive, anatomically real sculptures — a bronze 'David', the first free-standing nude since antiquity — set the standard for Renaissance sculpture and paved the way for Michelangelo.
cards.cue: The Florentine sculptor whose lifelike bronze 'David' revived classical statuary.

cards.back: Florentine painter of the early Renaissance, patronised by the Medici.
cards.extra: 1445–1510. His graceful mythological masterpieces — 'The Birth of Venus', 'Primavera' — embody the Renaissance revival of classical beauty and remain among the most famous images ever painted.
cards.why: His graceful mythological masterpieces — 'The Birth of Venus', 'Primavera' — embody the Renaissance revival of classical beauty and remain among the most famous images ever painted.
cards.cue: The Florentine whose 'Birth of Venus' and 'Primavera' epitomise Renaissance beauty.

cards.back: Italian painter, inventor and scientist, the ideal 'Renaissance man'.
cards.extra: 1452–1519. Painter of the 'Mona Lisa' and 'The Last Supper', he also filled notebooks with anatomy, engineering and flying machines centuries ahead of his time — the supreme embodiment of universal genius.
cards.why: Painter of the 'Mona Lisa' and 'The Last Supper', he also filled notebooks with anatomy, engineering and flying machines centuries ahead of his time — the supreme embodiment of universal genius.
cards.cue: The painter of the 'Mona Lisa' whose notebooks make him the ultimate 'Renaissance man'.

cards.back: Italian artist who created the 'David' and the Sistine Chapel ceiling.
cards.extra: 1475–1564. Master of sculpture ('David', 'Pietà'), painting (the Sistine Chapel) and architecture (St Peter's dome), he is often called the greatest artist who ever lived — the peak of the High Renaissance.
cards.why: Master of sculpture ('David', 'Pietà'), painting (the Sistine Chapel) and architecture (St Peter's dome), he is often called the greatest artist who ever lived — the peak of the High Renaissance.
cards.cue: The artist of the 'David' and the Sistine Chapel ceiling, often called the greatest ever.

cards.back: Italian painter, the third giant of the High Renaissance.
cards.extra: 1483–1520. Master of harmony and grace ('The School of Athens', his serene Madonnas), he distilled the Renaissance ideal of balanced beauty so perfectly that he set the standard for 'classical' art for centuries.
cards.why: Master of harmony and grace ('The School of Athens', his serene Madonnas), he distilled the Renaissance ideal of balanced beauty so perfectly that he set the standard for 'classical' art for centuries.
cards.cue: The High Renaissance painter of 'The School of Athens', the master of harmony and grace.

cards.back: The leading painter of the Venetian Renaissance.
cards.extra: c. 1488–1576. His revolutionary use of colour and loose brushwork — in portraits, myths and altarpieces — reshaped European painting and influenced everyone from Rubens to the Impressionists.
cards.why: His revolutionary use of colour and loose brushwork — in portraits, myths and altarpieces — reshaped European painting and influenced everyone from Rubens to the Impressionists.
cards.cue: The Venetian master whose revolutionary colour reshaped European painting.

cards.front: Pico della Mirandola — Renaissance humanist philosopher
cards.back: Italian humanist, author of the 'Oration on the Dignity of Man'.
cards.extra: 1463–1494. His 'Oration' — often called the manifesto of the Renaissance — proclaimed that humans are free to shape their own nature and rise toward the divine, the boldest statement of Renaissance humanism's faith in human potential.
cards.why: His 'Oration' — often called the manifesto of the Renaissance — proclaimed that humans are free to shape their own nature and rise toward the divine, the boldest statement of Renaissance humanism's faith in human potential.
cards.cue: The humanist whose 'Oration on the Dignity of Man' became the manifesto of the Renaissance.

cards.front: The Medici — Florentine banking dynasty & patrons
cards.back: The banking family who ruled Florence and funded the Renaissance.
cards.extra: 15th–16th c.. Cosimo and Lorenzo 'the Magnificent' turned their banking fortune into patronage of Leonardo, Michelangelo, Botticelli and Brunelleschi — proving that wealth wisely spent on art and learning can shape a whole civilisation; they produced popes and queens too.
cards.why: Cosimo and Lorenzo 'the Magnificent' turned their banking fortune into patronage of Leonardo, Michelangelo, Botticelli and Brunelleschi — proving that wealth wisely spent on art and learning can shape a whole civilisation; they produced popes and queens too.
cards.cue: The Florentine banking dynasty whose patronage bankrolled the Renaissance.

cards.back: Italian poet and scholar, 'the father of humanism'.
cards.extra: 1304–1374. By rediscovering and championing classical texts and writing intensely personal poetry (the sonnet), he launched Renaissance humanism — the rebirth of interest in the ancient world and the individual.
cards.why: By rediscovering and championing classical texts and writing intensely personal poetry (the sonnet), he launched Renaissance humanism — the rebirth of interest in the ancient world and the individual.
cards.cue: The poet and scholar called the 'father of humanism' who revived the classical world.

cards.back: Italian painter and architect who wrote the first history of art.
cards.extra: 1511–1574. His 'Lives of the Artists' chronicled the Renaissance masters, invented art history and the very idea of the 'Renaissance', and is the source of many stories we still tell of Leonardo and Michelangelo.
cards.why: His 'Lives of the Artists' chronicled the Renaissance masters, invented art history and the very idea of the 'Renaissance', and is the source of many stories we still tell of Leonardo and Michelangelo.
cards.cue: The painter whose 'Lives of the Artists' invented art history and the idea of the 'Renaissance'.

cards.back: Italian architect, the most influential in Western history.
cards.extra: 1508–1580. His serene, classically proportioned villas and his treatise spread 'Palladian' architecture across Europe and America — shaping everything from English country houses to the US Capitol and the White House.
cards.why: His serene, classically proportioned villas and his treatise spread 'Palladian' architecture across Europe and America — shaping everything from English country houses to the US Capitol and the White House.
cards.cue: The architect whose classical villas inspired everything from English manors to the White House.

cards.back: Flemish painter, a pioneer of oil painting and Northern Renaissance realism.
cards.extra: c. 1390–1441. He perfected oil paint and minute realism (the 'Arnolfini Portrait'), showing the North's own path to the Renaissance — obsessive detail and light rather than Italian idealism.
cards.why: He perfected oil paint and minute realism (the 'Arnolfini Portrait'), showing the North's own path to the Renaissance — obsessive detail and light rather than Italian idealism.
cards.cue: The Flemish master who perfected oil painting and dazzling realism in the North.

cards.back: German artist, the greatest of the Northern Renaissance.
cards.extra: 1471–1528. He fused Italian Renaissance ideas with Northern precision, and his engravings and woodcuts — spread by the new printing press — made him the first artist with a truly international reputation.
cards.why: He fused Italian Renaissance ideas with Northern precision, and his engravings and woodcuts — spread by the new printing press — made him the first artist with a truly international reputation.
cards.cue: The German master whose prints made him the Northern Renaissance's international star.

cards.back: Flemish painter of peasant life and landscapes.
cards.extra: c. 1525–1569. He turned art toward ordinary people and everyday life — harvests, weddings, proverbs, seasons — pioneering the landscape and genre scenes that would flower in Dutch and later European painting.
cards.why: He turned art toward ordinary people and everyday life — harvests, weddings, proverbs, seasons — pioneering the landscape and genre scenes that would flower in Dutch and later European painting.
cards.cue: The Flemish painter who turned art toward peasants, seasons and everyday life.

cards.back: Italian Baroque painter famed for dramatic light and dark.
cards.extra: 1571–1610. His violent realism and theatrical 'chiaroscuro' (extreme light and shadow) revolutionised European painting and launched the Baroque — as turbulent in art as he was in life.
cards.why: His violent realism and theatrical 'chiaroscuro' (extreme light and shadow) revolutionised European painting and launched the Baroque — as turbulent in art as he was in life.
cards.cue: The turbulent painter who launched the Baroque with extreme light and shadow.

cards.back: Dutch painter, the greatest of the Dutch Golden Age.
cards.extra: 1606–1669. Master of light, shadow and psychological depth — in portraits, biblical scenes and unflinching self-portraits — he is held to be one of the supreme painters and storytellers in the history of art.
cards.why: Master of light, shadow and psychological depth — in portraits, biblical scenes and unflinching self-portraits — he is held to be one of the supreme painters and storytellers in the history of art.
cards.cue: The Dutch Golden Age master of light, shadow and searching self-portraits.

cards.back: Dutch painter of luminous, quiet domestic scenes.
cards.extra: 1632–1675. His small, exquisite interiors ('Girl with a Pearl Earring') capture light and stillness with a precision that has made him, centuries later, one of the most beloved of all painters.
cards.why: His small, exquisite interiors ('Girl with a Pearl Earring') capture light and stillness with a precision that has made him, centuries later, one of the most beloved of all painters.
cards.cue: The Dutch painter of luminous quiet interiors, including 'Girl with a Pearl Earring'.

cards.back: Spanish painter, a bridge between the Old Masters and modern art.
cards.extra: 1746–1828. From glittering royal portraits to the nightmarish 'Black Paintings' and unflinching war scenes, he opened art to psychology, protest and the dark side of human nature — a father of modern art.
cards.why: From glittering royal portraits to the nightmarish 'Black Paintings' and unflinching war scenes, he opened art to psychology, protest and the dark side of human nature — a father of modern art.
cards.cue: The Spanish painter whose war scenes and 'Black Paintings' opened art to horror and protest.

cards.front: J. M. W. Turner — Romantic painter
cards.back: English Romantic painter of light, sea and storm.
cards.extra: 1775–1851. His radiant, near-abstract landscapes of light and weather pushed painting toward pure atmosphere and colour — anticipating Impressionism and modern art by decades.
cards.why: His radiant, near-abstract landscapes of light and weather pushed painting toward pure atmosphere and colour — anticipating Impressionism and modern art by decades.
cards.cue: The English Romantic whose luminous storms and seas anticipated Impressionism.

cards.back: French painter, the leading figure of Impressionism.
cards.extra: 1840–1926. His loose studies of light and moment (his 'Impression, Sunrise' gave the movement its name; the water lilies and haystacks) broke from realism toward capturing perception itself — the threshold of modern art.
cards.why: His loose studies of light and moment (his 'Impression, Sunrise' gave the movement its name; the water lilies and haystacks) broke from realism toward capturing perception itself — the threshold of modern art.
cards.cue: The painter whose 'Impression, Sunrise' named Impressionism, master of light and the moment.

cards.back: Dutch post-Impressionist painter, unrecognised in his own lifetime.
cards.extra: 1853–1890. His intense colour and swirling emotion ('The Starry Night') pioneered expression over realism; poor and troubled in life, he became after death the archetype of the misunderstood genius and one of the most loved painters ever.
cards.why: His intense colour and swirling emotion ('The Starry Night') pioneered expression over realism; poor and troubled in life, he became after death the archetype of the misunderstood genius and one of the most loved painters ever.
cards.cue: The tormented painter of 'The Starry Night', unknown in life and adored after death.

cards.back: Spanish artist, the most influential of the 20th century.
cards.extra: 1881–1973. Co-founder of Cubism, he shattered traditional perspective and reinvented what art could be again and again ('Guernica'), dominating modern art and becoming its defining figure.
cards.why: Co-founder of Cubism, he shattered traditional perspective and reinvented what art could be again and again ('Guernica'), dominating modern art and becoming its defining figure.
cards.cue: The Spanish co-founder of Cubism who dominated 20th-century art ('Guernica').

cards.back: The ancient Greek poet of the 'Iliad' and the 'Odyssey'.
cards.extra: c. 8th century BCE. His epics stand at the fountainhead of Western literature — the founding stories of Greek civilisation, endlessly retold, and the model of the heroic tale for 3,000 years.
cards.why: His epics stand at the fountainhead of Western literature — the founding stories of Greek civilisation, endlessly retold, and the model of the heroic tale for 3,000 years.
cards.cue: The ancient poet of the 'Iliad' and 'Odyssey' at the fountainhead of Western literature.

cards.back: Roman poet, author of the 'Aeneid'.
cards.extra: 70–19 BCE. His 'Aeneid' gave Rome its national epic and founding myth, becoming the model of Latin poetry and a bridge to the Christian Middle Ages — he guides Dante through Hell.
cards.why: His 'Aeneid' gave Rome its national epic and founding myth, becoming the model of Latin poetry and a bridge to the Christian Middle Ages — he guides Dante through Hell.
cards.cue: The Roman poet whose 'Aeneid' became Rome's national epic and Dante's guide.

cards.back: Italian poet, author of the 'Divine Comedy'.
cards.extra: 1265–1321. His journey through Hell, Purgatory and Paradise is the supreme poem of the Middle Ages; by writing it in Italian he helped forge the language and is called 'the father of the Italian language'.
cards.why: His journey through Hell, Purgatory and Paradise is the supreme poem of the Middle Ages; by writing it in Italian he helped forge the language and is called 'the father of the Italian language'.
cards.cue: The poet of the 'Divine Comedy' who fathered the Italian language.

cards.back: English poet, author of 'The Canterbury Tales'.
cards.extra: c. 1343–1400. The 'father of English literature', he wrote great poetry in English rather than French or Latin — his vivid 'Canterbury Tales' establishing English as a literary language.
cards.why: The 'father of English literature', he wrote great poetry in English rather than French or Latin — his vivid 'Canterbury Tales' establishing English as a literary language.
cards.cue: The 'father of English literature' whose 'Canterbury Tales' made English a literary language.

cards.back: Spanish writer, author of 'Don Quixote'.
cards.extra: 1547–1616. 'Don Quixote' is widely called the first modern novel and one of the greatest ever written — a funny, humane tale of an idealist tilting at windmills that founded Western fiction.
cards.why: 'Don Quixote' is widely called the first modern novel and one of the greatest ever written — a funny, humane tale of an idealist tilting at windmills that founded Western fiction.
cards.cue: The Spanish author of 'Don Quixote', often called the first modern novel.

cards.back: English playwright, the greatest writer in the English language.
cards.extra: 1564–1616. His plays and sonnets ('Hamlet', 'Macbeth', 'Romeo and Juliet') plumb human nature with unmatched depth and coined hundreds of everyday phrases — the towering figure of world literature.
cards.why: His plays and sonnets ('Hamlet', 'Macbeth', 'Romeo and Juliet') plumb human nature with unmatched depth and coined hundreds of everyday phrases — the towering figure of world literature.
cards.cue: The English playwright of 'Hamlet' and 'Macbeth', the greatest writer in the language.

cards.back: English poet, author of 'Paradise Lost'.
cards.extra: 1608–1674. His epic 'Paradise Lost' — the Fall of Man, and a strangely sympathetic Satan — is the greatest long poem in English and a monument of the language and of Protestant imagination.
cards.why: His epic 'Paradise Lost' — the Fall of Man, and a strangely sympathetic Satan — is the greatest long poem in English and a monument of the language and of Protestant imagination.
cards.cue: The blind poet whose epic 'Paradise Lost' retold the Fall of Man.

cards.back: French playwright, the master of comedy.
cards.extra: 1622–1673. His satirical comedies ('Tartuffe', 'The Misanthrope') skewered hypocrisy and pretension so brilliantly that French itself is sometimes called 'the language of Molière'.
cards.why: His satirical comedies ('Tartuffe', 'The Misanthrope') skewered hypocrisy and pretension so brilliantly that French itself is sometimes called 'the language of Molière'.
cards.cue: The French master of satirical comedy whose name stands for the French language itself.

cards.front: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe — Writer & polymath
cards.back: German writer and thinker, author of 'Faust'.
cards.extra: 1749–1832. The towering figure of German literature — poet, novelist, dramatist and scientist — his 'Faust', the man who sells his soul for knowledge, is a defining myth of the modern age.
cards.why: The towering figure of German literature — poet, novelist, dramatist and scientist — his 'Faust', the man who sells his soul for knowledge, is a defining myth of the modern age.
cards.cue: The giant of German literature whose 'Faust' sold his soul for knowledge.

cards.back: English novelist of manners and marriage.
cards.extra: 1775–1817. Her sharp, ironic novels ('Pride and Prejudice') perfected the novel of character and society and, centuries on, remain among the most read and adapted in the language.
cards.why: Her sharp, ironic novels ('Pride and Prejudice') perfected the novel of character and society and, centuries on, remain among the most read and adapted in the language.
cards.cue: The English novelist of 'Pride and Prejudice', mistress of irony and manners.

cards.back: French novelist and poet, author of 'Les Misérables'.
cards.extra: 1802–1885. The colossus of French Romanticism, his sweeping novels ('Les Misérables', 'The Hunchback of Notre-Dame') married great storytelling to a passionate conscience for the poor and oppressed.
cards.why: The colossus of French Romanticism, his sweeping novels ('Les Misérables', 'The Hunchback of Notre-Dame') married great storytelling to a passionate conscience for the poor and oppressed.
cards.cue: The French Romantic whose 'Les Misérables' married epic storytelling to social conscience.

cards.back: English novelist of the Victorian age.
cards.extra: 1812–1870. His vivid, sprawling novels ('Oliver Twist', 'A Christmas Carol', 'Great Expectations') exposed the injustices of industrial Britain and created unforgettable characters, making him the most popular novelist of his era.
cards.why: His vivid, sprawling novels ('Oliver Twist', 'A Christmas Carol', 'Great Expectations') exposed the injustices of industrial Britain and created unforgettable characters, making him the most popular novelist of his era.
cards.cue: The Victorian novelist of 'Oliver Twist' and 'A Christmas Carol' who exposed industrial Britain.

cards.back: Russian writer, author of 'War and Peace'.
cards.extra: 1828–1910. 'War and Peace' and 'Anna Karenina' are often named the greatest novels ever written — vast, deeply human panoramas of Russian society; his later pacifist philosophy influenced Gandhi.
cards.why: 'War and Peace' and 'Anna Karenina' are often named the greatest novels ever written — vast, deeply human panoramas of Russian society; his later pacifist philosophy influenced Gandhi.
cards.cue: The Russian author of 'War and Peace', often called the greatest novelist of all.

cards.back: Russian writer of psychological and philosophical novels.
cards.extra: 1821–1881. 'Crime and Punishment' and 'The Brothers Karamazov' probe guilt, faith, freedom and evil with unmatched intensity — foundational to existentialism and modern psychology.
cards.why: 'Crime and Punishment' and 'The Brothers Karamazov' probe guilt, faith, freedom and evil with unmatched intensity — foundational to existentialism and modern psychology.
cards.cue: The Russian novelist of 'Crime and Punishment' who probed guilt, faith and the human soul.

cards.back: American writer, 'the father of American literature'.
cards.extra: 1835–1910. 'Huckleberry Finn' captured the American voice, landscape and conscience — on race and freedom — as no one had, making him the founding figure of a distinctly American literature.
cards.why: 'Huckleberry Finn' captured the American voice, landscape and conscience — on race and freedom — as no one had, making him the founding figure of a distinctly American literature.
cards.cue: The American writer of 'Huckleberry Finn', the founding voice of American literature.

cards.back: Irish writer, a pioneer of literary modernism.
cards.extra: 1882–1941. 'Ulysses' revolutionised the novel with stream of consciousness and dense allusion, becoming the landmark of modernist literature and one of its most influential (and difficult) works.
cards.why: 'Ulysses' revolutionised the novel with stream of consciousness and dense allusion, becoming the landmark of modernist literature and one of its most influential (and difficult) works.
cards.cue: The Irish modernist whose 'Ulysses' revolutionised the novel with stream of consciousness.

cards.back: German Baroque composer, a supreme master of Western music.
cards.extra: 1685–1750. His vast output perfected harmony and counterpoint (the 'Well-Tempered Clavier', the 'Mass in B minor') — the foundation on which all later Western classical music was built.
cards.why: His vast output perfected harmony and counterpoint (the 'Well-Tempered Clavier', the 'Mass in B minor') — the foundation on which all later Western classical music was built.
cards.cue: The Baroque master whose harmony and counterpoint underpin all later Western classical music.

cards.back: Austrian composer, a prodigy and one of the greatest ever.
cards.extra: 1756–1791. A child prodigy who wrote sublime works in every form — symphonies, operas ('The Magic Flute'), concertos — with matchless grace before dying at 35, the very ideal of natural genius.
cards.why: A child prodigy who wrote sublime works in every form — symphonies, operas ('The Magic Flute'), concertos — with matchless grace before dying at 35, the very ideal of natural genius.
cards.cue: The Austrian prodigy of matchless melody who wrote in every form and died at 35.

cards.back: German composer who bridged the Classical and Romantic eras.
cards.extra: 1770–1827. He expanded music's emotional power and scale (the Fifth and Ninth Symphonies) and — famously, while going deaf — turned it into personal, heroic expression, reshaping what music could mean.
cards.why: He expanded music's emotional power and scale (the Fifth and Ninth Symphonies) and — famously, while going deaf — turned it into personal, heroic expression, reshaping what music could mean.
cards.cue: The composer who, going deaf, wrote the Fifth and Ninth Symphonies and made music heroic.

cards.back: Polish composer and pianist of the Romantic era.
cards.extra: 1810–1849. He wrote almost entirely for the piano, perfecting its poetic, expressive voice (nocturnes, études, polonaises) — the soul of Romantic piano music.
cards.why: He wrote almost entirely for the piano, perfecting its poetic, expressive voice (nocturnes, études, polonaises) — the soul of Romantic piano music.
cards.cue: The Romantic composer who made the piano sing in nocturnes and polonaises.

cards.back: German composer who revolutionised opera.
cards.extra: 1813–1883. His epic music-dramas (the 'Ring' cycle) fused music, drama and myth into a 'total work of art', transforming opera and harmony — hugely influential, and controversial for his antisemitism and later Nazi association.
cards.why: His epic music-dramas (the 'Ring' cycle) fused music, drama and myth into a 'total work of art', transforming opera and harmony — hugely influential, and controversial for his antisemitism and later Nazi association.
cards.cue: The composer whose epic 'Ring' cycle reinvented opera as a 'total work of art'.

cards.back: Italian composer, the master of opera.
cards.extra: 1813–1901. His dramatic, deeply melodic operas ('La Traviata', 'Aida', 'Rigoletto') are among the most performed in the world, and he became a symbol of Italian national feeling during unification.
cards.why: His dramatic, deeply melodic operas ('La Traviata', 'Aida', 'Rigoletto') are among the most performed in the world, and he became a symbol of Italian national feeling during unification.
cards.cue: The Italian master of opera behind 'La Traviata' and 'Aida'.

cards.back: Ming Chinese admiral who led vast treasure-fleet voyages.
cards.extra: 1371–1433. Decades before Columbus, he commanded enormous fleets across the Indian Ocean to Africa, projecting China's power and trade — until China turned inward and abandoned the seas, a fateful road not taken.
cards.why: Decades before Columbus, he commanded enormous fleets across the Indian Ocean to Africa, projecting China's power and trade — until China turned inward and abandoned the seas, a fateful road not taken.
cards.cue: The Ming admiral whose giant fleets reached Africa decades before Columbus sailed.

cards.back: Venetian merchant whose travels to China entranced Europe.
cards.extra: 1254–1324. His account of years at Kublai Khan's court opened European eyes to the wealth of Asia, inspiring later explorers — including Columbus, who carried a copy — to seek routes east.
cards.why: His account of years at Kublai Khan's court opened European eyes to the wealth of Asia, inspiring later explorers — including Columbus, who carried a copy — to seek routes east.
cards.cue: The Venetian traveller whose tales of Kublai Khan's China inspired the Age of Exploration.

cards.back: Moroccan scholar who travelled further than anyone of his age.
cards.extra: 1304–1369. Over 30 years he journeyed some 120,000 km across Africa, the Middle East, India and China — his 'Rihla' the greatest travelogue of the medieval world and a window on the whole Islamic world.
cards.why: Over 30 years he journeyed some 120,000 km across Africa, the Middle East, India and China — his 'Rihla' the greatest travelogue of the medieval world and a window on the whole Islamic world.
cards.cue: The medieval Moroccan who travelled 120,000 km across the Islamic world and beyond.

cards.back: Genoese explorer whose 1492 voyage reached the Americas.
cards.extra: 1451–1506. Sailing west for Spain, he opened permanent contact between the Old World and the New — launching European colonisation of the Americas and the 'Columbian Exchange', for Europe a triumph and for native peoples a catastrophe.
cards.why: Sailing west for Spain, he opened permanent contact between the Old World and the New — launching European colonisation of the Americas and the 'Columbian Exchange', for Europe a triumph and for native peoples a catastrophe.
cards.cue: The explorer whose 1492 voyage opened the Americas to Europe — and to conquest.

cards.back: Portuguese explorer, first to sail from Europe to India.
cards.extra: c. 1460s–1524. By rounding Africa to reach India by sea (1498) he opened the ocean spice route, founding Portugal's Asian empire and Europe's age of maritime trade and colonisation in the East.
cards.why: By rounding Africa to reach India by sea (1498) he opened the ocean spice route, founding Portugal's Asian empire and Europe's age of maritime trade and colonisation in the East.
cards.cue: The Portuguese navigator who opened the sea route from Europe to India.

cards.back: Portuguese explorer who led the first circumnavigation of the globe.
cards.extra: c. 1480–1521. His expedition first sailed all the way around the world (1519–22), proving the Earth's size and one connected ocean — and though he died en route, the voyage remade humanity's picture of the planet.
cards.why: His expedition first sailed all the way around the world (1519–22), proving the Earth's size and one connected ocean — and though he died en route, the voyage remade humanity's picture of the planet.
cards.cue: The explorer whose expedition first sailed all the way around the world.

cards.back: British navigator who charted the Pacific.
cards.extra: 1728–1779. His three voyages mapped New Zealand, eastern Australia and much of the Pacific with new precision, opening the last great region to European contact — and colonisation.
cards.why: His three voyages mapped New Zealand, eastern Australia and much of the Pacific with new precision, opening the last great region to European contact — and colonisation.
cards.cue: The British navigator whose voyages charted the Pacific, New Zealand and Australia.

cards.back: German inventor of the movable-type printing press.
cards.extra: c. 1400–1468. His press (c. 1440) made books cheap and abundant for the first time — spreading the Renaissance, Reformation and Scientific Revolution and democratising knowledge, arguably the most consequential invention of the last millennium.
cards.why: His press (c. 1440) made books cheap and abundant for the first time — spreading the Renaissance, Reformation and Scientific Revolution and democratising knowledge, arguably the most consequential invention of the last millennium.
cards.cue: The inventor whose printing press made books cheap and spread knowledge across Europe.

cards.back: Scottish engineer who perfected the steam engine.
cards.extra: 1736–1819. His vastly improved steam engine powered the Industrial Revolution — factories, railways and ships — transforming how humans work and live; the unit of power, the watt, bears his name.
cards.why: His vastly improved steam engine powered the Industrial Revolution — factories, railways and ships — transforming how humans work and live; the unit of power, the watt, bears his name.
cards.cue: The engineer whose improved steam engine powered the Industrial Revolution.

cards.back: American industrialist who pioneered mass production.
cards.extra: 1863–1947. His moving assembly line made the car (the Model T) affordable for ordinary people, creating the modern consumer economy and the mass-production methods that define industry to this day.
cards.why: His moving assembly line made the car (the Model T) affordable for ordinary people, creating the modern consumer economy and the mass-production methods that define industry to this day.
cards.cue: The industrialist whose moving assembly line made the car affordable and pioneered mass production.

cards.front: What actually gives modern money its value?
cards.back: Nothing physical. It is 'fiat' money — it has value only because a government declares it legal tender and because people trust and accept it. It is no longer backed by gold.
cards.extra: The US dollar's last formal tie to gold was cut in 1971 (the 'Nixon shock'). Money is, at bottom, a shared social agreement that serves three jobs: a medium of exchange, a unit of account, and a store of value.

cards.front: How do commercial banks 'create' most of the money in the economy?
cards.back: By lending. When a bank makes a loan it credits the borrower's account with new deposits — it does not simply pass on cash someone else deposited. This is 'fractional-reserve' banking.
cards.extra: Most money in circulation is bank-deposit money created this way, not notes and coins printed by the state. It is also why a 'bank run' is dangerous — a bank never holds all of its depositors' money in the vault at once.

cards.front: What is a central bank, and what is its core job? (the Fed, the ECB, the Bank of England)
cards.back: A country's or currency zone's monetary authority. Its core jobs: set interest rates to keep inflation low and stable, issue the currency, and act as 'lender of last resort' to banks in a crisis.
cards.extra: Most aim for about 2% annual inflation. Examples: the US Federal Reserve ('the Fed'), the European Central Bank (ECB), the Bank of England. They are usually kept independent of day-to-day politics.

cards.front: What does it mean that the US dollar is the world's 'reserve currency'?
cards.back: It is the currency most used for global trade, debt and the reserves that central banks hold — roughly 58% of the world's foreign-exchange reserves (IMF, 2024), far ahead of the euro.
cards.extra: This gives the US great power: it can borrow cheaply and can impose financial sanctions by cutting countries off from dollar payments (e.g. SWIFT). Cross-links Geopolitics (financial power) and the 1944 Bretton Woods order (World Overview deck).

cards.front: Where does inflation ultimately come from?
cards.back: Broadly, too much money and demand chasing too few goods — classically, when the money supply grows much faster than the economy's output of real goods and services, prices rise.
cards.extra: Milton Friedman's line: 'inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon.' Supply shocks (e.g. an oil-price spike) also push prices up. The usual cure is higher interest rates, which cool demand.

cards.front: What is hyperinflation, and what causes it?
cards.back: Inflation so rapid that money loses value by the day — typically when a government prints money to cover spending it cannot otherwise fund. Prices can double in days.
cards.extra: Classic cases: Weimar Germany (1923), Zimbabwe (2008), Venezuela (2010s). It wipes out savings and destroys trust in the currency. (Cross-links Venezuela in the South America deck and Weimar in History.)

cards.back: When interest rates are already near zero, a central bank creates new money electronically and uses it to buy bonds and other assets — pushing money into the economy and holding down longer-term interest rates.
cards.extra: 'Unconventional' monetary policy, used heavily after the 2008 crisis and again in 2020. Its reverse — selling those assets and withdrawing the money — is 'quantitative tightening' (QT). (Cross-links the 2008 crisis card.)

cards.front: What was the gold standard, and why did the world abandon it?
cards.back: A system in which money was redeemable for a fixed amount of gold, which capped how much governments could print. It was given up in stages — finally in 1971 — for the flexibility to manage the economy.
cards.extra: The 1944 Bretton Woods system pegged currencies to the US dollar and the dollar to gold; the 1971 'Nixon shock' cut that link, ushering in today's fiat, floating currencies. The trade-off: hard-money discipline vs. policy flexibility. (Cross-links the reserve-currency and World decks.)

cards.front: What is deflation, and why can it be even more dangerous than inflation?
cards.back: A sustained FALL in the general price level. It sounds good, but it makes people delay spending (why buy now if it will be cheaper later?), makes existing debts heavier, and can trap an economy in a downward spiral.
