cards.extra: Japan's 'lost decades' from the 1990s and the Great Depression are the warnings. Central banks fear it partly because their main tool — cutting rates — runs out of room once rates reach zero.

cards.front: What is deposit insurance, and why does it make modern bank runs rare?
cards.back: A government guarantee of your bank deposits up to a set limit, so savers have no reason to panic-withdraw if a bank looks shaky — it defuses the self-fulfilling panic of a bank run.
cards.extra: Limits (2025): the US FDIC covers $250,000 per depositor, per bank; the UK's FSCS covers £120,000 (raised from £85,000 on 1 Dec 2025). Both were born from waves of bank failures — the FDIC after the Great Depression. (Cross-links the bank-run and 2008 cards.)

cards.front: What is GDP, and what does it measure?
cards.back: Gross Domestic Product — the total value of all goods and services a country produces in a year. It is the standard measure of the size of an economy.
cards.extra: World GDP is roughly $110 trillion (2024). GDP per person (per capita) is the rough proxy for average living standards — which is why the Geography decks show it. It famously ignores unpaid work, inequality and environmental damage.

cards.back: A significant, broad decline in economic activity — commonly defined as two consecutive quarters of falling GDP. Output shrinks and unemployment rises.
cards.extra: A recession is the down-phase of the 'business cycle' (the economy's recurring expansion and contraction). A deep, prolonged one is a depression — e.g. the Great Depression of the 1930s. (Cross-links History.)

cards.front: What is the difference between fiscal and monetary policy?
cards.back: Fiscal policy is the government's taxing and spending (run by the treasury / parliament). Monetary policy is the central bank's control of interest rates and the money supply.
cards.extra: They are the two main levers for steering the economy — different actors, different tools. In a downturn: fiscal stimulus = spend more / tax less; monetary stimulus = cut interest rates.

cards.front: Why is the interest rate the single most important lever in the economy?
cards.back: It is the price of borrowing money. Raising rates makes borrowing dearer, cooling spending and inflation; cutting rates makes it cheaper, stimulating activity.
cards.extra: When central banks 'raise' or 'cut rates', this is the lever they mean. Almost everything responds to it — mortgages, business investment, share prices and currencies.

cards.front: What does the unemployment rate measure — and what is the catch?
cards.back: The share of people who want a job and are actively looking but cannot find one. It is NOT everyone without a job — students, retirees and people not looking are not counted.
cards.extra: So a falling rate can mislead: people who give up looking drop out of the count entirely. Economists watch the 'participation rate' (how many are in the workforce at all) alongside it.

cards.front: What is the difference between a government's deficit and its debt?
cards.back: The deficit is how much MORE a government spends than it raises in a single year. The debt is the total accumulated from all past deficits — the running total it owes.
cards.extra: Each year's deficit adds to the debt; a surplus (raising more than you spend) shrinks it. Confusing the two is one of the most common economic errors in the news.

cards.front: How do governments actually raise most of their money?
cards.back: Mainly from a few big taxes: income tax (on wages), payroll / social-security contributions, taxes on spending (VAT or sales tax), and corporation tax on company profits.
cards.extra: In most rich countries income, payroll and VAT dominate; tariffs on imports are minor today. The broad splits: 'direct' taxes on income/wealth vs. 'indirect' taxes on spending — and progressive (a higher rate on higher incomes) vs. flat vs. regressive.

cards.front: What is the difference between 'real' and 'nominal' figures — and why does it matter?
cards.back: Nominal is the raw number; real is adjusted for inflation. A 3% pay rise when inflation is 5% is actually a real pay CUT — the money buys less than before.
cards.extra: Always ask 'real or nominal?' — it applies to wages, GDP growth and investment returns alike. It is the single most useful habit for not being fooled by inflation.

cards.front: What makes a currency's exchange rate rise or fall?
cards.back: Mostly supply and demand for the currency: trade flows, interest rates (higher rates attract foreign money and strengthen the currency) and confidence in the country. Most currencies 'float' freely; some are pegged/fixed to another (often the dollar).
cards.extra: A 'strong' currency makes imports cheaper but a country's exports dearer — so it is not simply good or bad. (Cross-links the reserve-currency card.)

cards.back: By tracking the price of a fixed 'basket' of typical goods and services over time — the Consumer Price Index (CPI). Inflation is the percentage change in that basket's cost, usually year-on-year.
cards.extra: So '3% inflation' means the basket costs 3% more than a year ago. Statisticians choose and weight the basket and update it as habits change — which is why measured inflation is a careful estimate, not an exact truth.

cards.front: What is the basic difference between a stock and a bond?
cards.back: A stock (share / equity) is part-ownership of a company — you share in its profits and its risk. A bond is a loan (debt) — you lend money for fixed interest and get the sum back at the end.
cards.extra: Equity = ownership: higher risk, higher potential reward. Debt = lending: safer and more predictable. These are the two fundamental ways to fund a company or a government.

cards.front: Why do bond prices fall when interest rates rise?
cards.back: Because new bonds are issued paying the higher rate, so an older bond paying the lower rate is worth less — its price drops until its return matches the market. Price and yield move inversely.
cards.extra: This inverse relationship is foundational to finance. It is why rising central-bank rates hurt existing bond holders, and why 'the bond market' reacts so sharply to rate news.

cards.front: What is a stock index, and what does it tell you? (the S&P 500, the Dow, the FTSE)
cards.back: A basket that tracks the share prices of many companies at once, so a single number summarises how a whole market is doing — e.g. the S&P 500 (500 large US companies).
cards.extra: Others: the Dow Jones and Nasdaq (US, tech-heavy), the FTSE 100 (UK), the Nikkei (Japan). 'The market was up today' usually means an index like these rose.

cards.front: What is an index fund, and why was it revolutionary?
cards.back: A fund that simply buys an entire index (e.g. all 500 S&P stocks) instead of trying to pick winners. It is cheap, passive, and spreads risk across the whole market.
cards.extra: Most active stock-pickers fail to beat the index over time, so this low-cost 'just buy everything' approach (pioneered by John Bogle, 1976) reshaped investing. (A concept to understand — not financial advice.)

cards.front: What does diversification mean, and why does it work?
cards.back: Spreading money across many different assets so that no single failure can wipe you out — 'don't put all your eggs in one basket.' Independent risks partly cancel out.
cards.extra: It is the closest thing to a free lunch in finance: it can lower risk without necessarily lowering expected return. It is the logic behind index funds and balanced portfolios.

cards.front: What is the yield curve, and why do people watch for an 'inversion'?
cards.back: A graph of interest rates on government bonds across time horizons. Normally longer-term bonds pay more; when short-term rates rise ABOVE long-term ones (an 'inversion'), it has historically tended to precede recessions.
cards.extra: An inverted curve signals that markets expect the central bank to cut rates soon — i.e. they expect a slowdown. It is one of the most-watched recession warning signs.

cards.front: What is a stock exchange, and how do shares come to be traded on one?
cards.back: A marketplace (e.g. the NYSE, Nasdaq, London Stock Exchange) where shares are bought and sold. A company first sells shares to the public in an 'IPO' (initial public offering) to raise money; after that, investors trade those shares among themselves.
cards.extra: 'Primary market' = the company raising money by issuing shares; 'secondary market' = investors trading existing shares. A company's 'market cap' = share price × number of shares = the market's price tag for the whole company.

cards.back: Higher potential returns come only with higher risk. Safe assets (cash, government bonds) pay little; risky ones (stocks, startups) may pay far more — or lose you everything. There is no high-return, no-risk asset.
cards.extra: The extra return investors demand for taking on risk is the 'risk premium'. If anyone promises high returns with no risk, treat it as a red flag for fraud. (Cross-links the Ponzi-scheme card.)

cards.front: What is a fund (a mutual fund or ETF), and why do most people invest through one?
cards.back: A pooled investment holding many assets at once: you buy a single share of the fund and own a slice of everything in it — instant diversification. An ETF trades on an exchange all day like a stock; a traditional mutual fund is priced once a day.
cards.extra: Most index funds today are ETFs. Funds are how ordinary savers hold shares without buying dozens of stocks individually. (A concept to understand — not financial advice.)

cards.front: What is the 'time value of money'?
cards.back: A dollar today is worth more than a dollar a year from now — because today's dollar can be invested to earn interest, and because inflation erodes the value of future money.
cards.extra: It is the foundation of all finance: interest rates, bond prices and the valuation of whole companies are all ways of 'discounting' future money back to what it is worth today. (Cross-links compound interest.)

cards.front: What is the law of supply and demand?
cards.back: Price settles where the two meet: if something is scarce and wanted, its price rises; if it is abundant or unwanted, the price falls — and those price moves push supply and demand back toward balance.
cards.extra: The most basic mechanism in economics. A price is information — it tells producers what to make and buyers what is scarce, coordinating millions of people with no one in charge.

cards.back: The value of the next-best thing you give up when you make a choice. The true cost of anything is what you forgo to get it — not just the money you hand over.
cards.extra: Perhaps the most useful economic idea for everyday thinking: nothing is truly 'free' — an hour or a dollar spent here is one not spent there. 'There is no such thing as a free lunch.'

cards.front: What is comparative advantage, and why does it justify trade?
cards.back: Even if one country is better at making everything, BOTH gain if each specialises in what it is RELATIVELY best at and then trades. Total output rises.
cards.extra: David Ricardo's counter-intuitive insight (1817) — the core economic case for free trade. It turns on relative, not absolute, efficiency. (Cross-links the trade cards in Geopolitics.)

cards.front: What did Adam Smith mean by the 'invisible hand'?
cards.back: That individuals pursuing their own self-interest in a free market are led, as if by an invisible hand, to outcomes that benefit society as a whole — without any of them intending it.
cards.extra: From The Wealth of Nations (1776), the founding idea of market economics: the butcher sells you meat from self-interest, not kindness — and you are fed regardless. (Cross-links the future World Literacy::History::People deck.)

cards.front: What is the core disagreement between Keynesian and free-market (monetarist) economics?
cards.back: Keynesians argue markets can get stuck in slumps, so government should spend to revive demand. Monetarists / free-market economists argue government should mostly stand back and steer the money supply; markets self-correct.
cards.extra: John Maynard Keynes (1930s, the Depression) versus Milton Friedman (later 20th century). How far the state should intervene still divides economic policy today. (Cross-links the future World Literacy::History::People deck.)

cards.front: What is compound interest, and why is it so powerful?
cards.back: Earning interest on your interest, not just on the original sum — so growth speeds up over time. Money grows exponentially, not in a straight line.
cards.extra: Often called the eighth wonder of the world. A modest rate compounded over decades produces surprisingly large sums — and it works in reverse for debt (e.g. credit cards). 'Rule of 72': divide 72 by the % rate to get the years to double.

cards.front: Why do economists say 'incentives are everything'?
cards.back: Because people respond to incentives — change the rewards or penalties and you change behaviour, often in ways no one intended. Much of economics is the study of how incentives shape what people actually do.
cards.extra: Beware 'perverse incentives': a colonial bounty on dead cobras led people to BREED cobras for the reward (the 'cobra effect'). Taxes, subsidies, prices and laws all work by changing incentives.

cards.back: A cost or benefit from an activity that falls on third parties and is not reflected in its price. Pollution is the classic negative externality; education and vaccination are positive ones.
cards.extra: It is a key reason markets can 'fail': a factory that can pollute for free will overproduce pollution. Fixes try to 'internalise' the cost — e.g. a carbon tax or regulation. (Cross-links the Global Trends emissions card.)

cards.front: What is the 'tragedy of the commons'?
cards.back: When a shared, open-access resource is overused and ruined because each user reaps the full benefit of taking more while the cost is spread across everyone — e.g. overfishing, deforestation, a warming climate.
cards.extra: Named by Garrett Hardin (1968). Solutions: property rights, government regulation, or community management of the resource (the work of Elinor Ostrom). (Cross-links the climate cards in Global Trends.)

cards.front: What did Schumpeter mean by 'creative destruction'?
cards.back: That capitalism grows by constantly destroying old industries as it creates new ones — cars replaced horse-carriages, smartphones replaced cameras and music players. The churn is painful but is the engine of progress.
cards.extra: Joseph Schumpeter (1942). It captures both the dynamism of market economies and the disruption and job losses that innovation inevitably brings.

cards.front: What is behavioural economics, and how does it break from classic theory?
cards.back: It studies how people REALLY decide — predictably irrationally — rather than as the perfectly rational 'economic man' of older theory. A signature finding is 'loss aversion': losing $100 hurts more than gaining $100 feels good.
cards.extra: Built on the psychology of Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky (Kahneman won the 2002 Nobel); Richard Thaler carried it into economics and the idea of 'nudges' (2017 Nobel). (A natural cross-link to the future History::People deck.)

cards.back: When the price of an asset is driven far above its real worth by speculation — people buying mainly because prices are rising and they expect to sell to someone else for more — until confidence cracks and the price collapses.
cards.extra: Bubbles follow a recurring pattern (boom → euphoria → panic → crash) and recur across centuries, because the underlying driver is human psychology, not any one particular asset.

cards.front: What was 'Tulip mania', and why is it remembered?
cards.back: A speculative frenzy in 1630s Holland in which the prices of rare tulip bulbs soared to extraordinary heights and then collapsed in 1637. It is often cited as the first well-documented asset bubble.
cards.extra: At the peak, single bulbs reportedly changed hands for more than the price of a house. (Some historians argue the episode was exaggerated, but it endures as the archetype of a mania.)

cards.front: What happened in the Wall Street Crash of 1929 and the Great Depression?
cards.back: The 1929 US stock-market crash helped tip the world into the deepest depression of modern times: US unemployment reached about 25%, thousands of banks failed, and global trade collapsed through the 1930s.
cards.extra: It reshaped economics (Keynes's case for government action) and policy (deposit insurance, the central bank as lender of last resort, the welfare state). (Cross-links the recession and Keynes cards.)

cards.front: What caused the 2008 Global Financial Crisis?
cards.back: Risky US home loans ('subprime' mortgages) were bundled into complex securities and sold worldwide. When US house prices fell and borrowers defaulted, those securities collapsed; the failure of the bank Lehman Brothers in September 2008 froze the global financial system.
cards.extra: The worst crisis since the 1930s — the 'Great Recession'. Governments bailed out banks and central banks launched QE; new rules followed (Dodd-Frank in the US, the global Basel III). (Cross-links the QE card.)

cards.front: What do 'too big to fail' and 'moral hazard' mean?
cards.back: Some banks are so central that letting them collapse would crash the whole economy, so governments rescue them. But knowing they will be bailed out encourages reckless risk-taking — that distorted incentive is 'moral hazard'.
cards.extra: It was the central dilemma of 2008: rescue reckless banks (and reward failure) or let them fail (and risk a depression). (Cross-links the incentives card.)

cards.back: A fraud that pays existing investors with money from new investors rather than from any real profit. It looks like a brilliant, steady investment — until new money dries up and the whole thing collapses.
cards.extra: Named after Charles Ponzi (1920). Bernie Madoff ran the largest in history (~$65 billion), exposed in December 2008. The red flag is always the same: high, steady, 'risk-free' returns. (Cross-links the risk–return card.)

cards.front: Why can even a healthy-looking bank collapse in a matter of days?
cards.back: Because a bank lends out most of the money deposited with it, so it cannot repay all depositors at once. If enough customers demand their money together — a 'bank run' — the bank fails. In the digital age this can happen in hours.
cards.extra: Silicon Valley Bank collapsed in 2023 after roughly $42 billion was withdrawn in a single day — the biggest US bank failure since 2008. Deposit insurance and the central bank as lender of last resort exist to stop such panics. (Cross-links fractional-reserve banking and deposit insurance.)

cards.back: A frenzy around 1995–2000 in which investors poured money into internet companies with little or no profit. The tech-heavy Nasdaq index peaked in March 2000 and then fell about 78% by late 2002.
cards.extra: Countless 'eyeballs-not-earnings' startups vanished, while a few survivors (such as Amazon) went on to dominate. The lesson: a genuine technological revolution can still be wildly over-priced in the short run.

cards.front: What is a central bank's job in a financial panic — the 'lender of last resort'?
cards.back: To lend freely to solvent banks during a panic, so that a temporary loss of confidence does not snowball into a wave of collapses. The central bank is the financial system's ultimate backstop.
cards.extra: The classic rule (Walter Bagehot, 1873): in a crisis, lend freely, at a penalty rate, against good collateral. Central banks played exactly this role in 2008 and in 2020. (Cross-links the central-bank card.)

cards.front: Which nine states possess nuclear weapons?
cards.back: The US, Russia, China, France, the UK, India, Pakistan, Israel and North Korea.
cards.extra: About 12,200 warheads in all. Russia (~5,500) and the US (~5,200) together hold ~86% — far more than the other seven combined. (The first five are also the UN Security Council P5.)

cards.front: Two countries hold the overwhelming majority of the world's nuclear warheads — which, and which is rising fastest?
cards.back: Russia and the United States — together about 86% of the global arsenal.
cards.extra: China is a distant third (~600 warheads) but is growing fastest, adding roughly 100 a year. France (~290) and the UK (~225) follow; India, Pakistan, Israel and North Korea have fewer.

cards.front: Who are the world's biggest military spenders?
cards.back: The United States — by a huge margin ($997 billion in 2024, ~3.2× the next) — then China (~$314 bn) and Russia (~$149 bn).
cards.extra: The top five (US, China, Russia, Germany, India) are about 60% of the world's ~$2.7 trillion in military spending (SIPRI, 2024).

cards.front: Which countries have the largest armed forces by number of personnel?
cards.back: China and India (each over a million), then the United States, North Korea and Russia.
cards.extra: Troop numbers are not the same as power: the US spends the most and has by far the most advanced military, while North Korea fields a huge but poorly-equipped army.

cards.front: Beyond NATO, name some of the other major security or power blocs.
cards.back: The CSTO (Russia-led), the SCO (China + Russia + Central/South Asia), AUKUS (US–UK–Australia, 2021) and the Quad (US, Japan, India, Australia).
cards.extra: AUKUS and the Quad are explicitly aimed at balancing China in the Indo-Pacific; the CSTO and SCO orbit Moscow and Beijing.

cards.front: Why do the SAME five countries keep appearing — UN vetoes, the biggest nuclear arsenals, top military spenders?
cards.back: Because the US, Russia, China, the UK and France are the victors of World War II, who built their privileged status into the post-war order — the Security Council veto and (via the NPT) recognised nuclear status.
cards.extra: It's the clearest case of 'history shapes the present': 1945's winners still sit at the head of the table. (Cross-links the History and World Overview decks.)

cards.front: Why are there exactly nine nuclear states — and what is the NPT?
cards.back: The 1968 Non-Proliferation Treaty froze the 'nuclear club': the five who already had the bomb (US, Russia, UK, France, China — the UN Security Council P5) are recognised as nuclear powers; everyone else signs away the right to build one, in return for help with civil nuclear energy.
cards.extra: Four states stayed outside or broke the treaty — India, Pakistan, Israel and North Korea — which is why the club is nine, not five. The bargain is fraying, with Iran's programme the central worry. (Cross-links the History 1945-order card and the P5.)

cards.front: What is nuclear deterrence — and 'mutually assured destruction'?
cards.back: The idea that nuclear weapons prevent war by making it suicidal: if any attack would trigger a devastating nuclear response, no rational state starts one. When both sides can annihilate each other, that stalemate is Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD).
cards.extra: It's why the Cold War stayed 'cold' — the superpowers never fought directly. The flip side: it assumes rational actors and raises terrifying stakes around miscalculation (see the Cuban Missile Crisis). (Cross-links the History Cold War cards.)

cards.front: What is NATO, and what makes it work?
cards.back: The North Atlantic Treaty Organisation (1949) — a military alliance of 32 mostly Western states (the US, Canada and most of Europe). Its heart is Article 5: an armed attack on one member is treated as an attack on all.
cards.extra: That collective-defence promise, backed by US power, is what deterred the USSR in the Cold War. NATO expanded eastward after 1991, and Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine pushed long-neutral Finland and Sweden to join. (Cross-links the History 'Founding of NATO' card.)

cards.front: What are the SCO and the CSTO — the blocs that orbit Moscow and Beijing?
cards.back: The CSTO is a Russia-led mutual-defence pact of a few post-Soviet states (a mini-NATO for Moscow). The SCO is a larger China- and Russia-led security and political group spanning Central and South Asia (India, Pakistan and Iran are members).
cards.extra: Neither has NATO's binding cohesion — the SCO especially is less an alliance than a loose Eurasian counterweight to Western influence. (Cross-links Russia and China in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: What are AUKUS and the Quad, and what are they for?
cards.back: Both are US-led groupings meant to balance China in the Indo-Pacific. AUKUS (2021: US, UK, Australia) is a defence-technology pact centred on nuclear-powered submarines for Australia; the Quad (US, Japan, India, Australia) is a looser strategic dialogue.
cards.extra: Neither is a formal mutual-defence alliance like NATO — they exist to knit together China's wary neighbours. India's role in the Quad is striking given its long tradition of non-alignment. (Cross-links the East Asia and South Asia decks.)

cards.front: Where does the world's electricity actually come from?
cards.back: Still mostly fossil fuels: coal ~34%, gas ~22% (fossil ~59% in total); then hydro ~14%, nuclear ~9%, wind ~8% and solar ~7%.
cards.extra: 2024 was the first year since the 1940s that fossil fuels fell below 60% of electricity. Solar is the fastest-growing source by far (Ember, 2024).

cards.front: Who are the world's top oil producers?
cards.back: The United States (#1 by a wide margin, on shale), then Saudi Arabia and Russia.
cards.extra: Saudi Arabia and Russia anchor the OPEC+ group that manages output to steer prices. The key oil chokepoint is the Strait of Hormuz (see Trade & Seas).

cards.front: Which country has the largest proven oil reserves — and is it the biggest producer?
cards.back: Venezuela has the largest proven reserves, ahead of Saudi Arabia — but it is NOT a big producer (its industry collapsed). The US produces the most.
cards.extra: A 'structure & exception' fact: reserves in the ground are not the same as oil actually pumped. (Cross-links Venezuela in the South America deck.)

cards.front: What is the 'energy transition', and where does it stand?
cards.back: The shift from fossil fuels to low-carbon power (solar, wind, hydro, nuclear). Clean sources passed ~41% of the world's electricity in 2024 and are rising fast.
cards.extra: But total energy demand keeps growing, so global emissions are still rising — the same honest exception as the Global Trends deck's greenhouse-gas card.

cards.front: Why is natural gas more of a geopolitical lever than oil — and who are the big players?
cards.back: Gas is hard to move (it needs fixed pipelines or costly LNG terminals), so it ties specific suppliers and buyers tightly together. Top producers: the US and Russia; top LNG exporters: the US, Qatar and Australia.
cards.extra: Russia's pipeline gas to Europe was a major source of leverage — until Europe scrambled to cut it off after the 2022 invasion of Ukraine.

cards.front: Which single waterway is the world's most important oil chokepoint?
cards.back: The Strait of Hormuz — between Iran and Oman, at the mouth of the Persian Gulf. Roughly a fifth of the world's oil passes through it.
cards.extra: Its narrowness gives Iran outsized leverage; any threat to close it spikes global oil prices.

cards.front: Roughly what share of world trade travels by sea — and in what?
cards.back: Around 80–90% of world trade by volume goes by ship, most of it in standardised steel shipping containers.
cards.extra: Containerisation (from the 1950s) collapsed the cost of moving goods and is one of the great unsung engines of globalisation.

cards.front: What are the world's busiest container ports?
cards.back: Shanghai (#1, ~50 million containers a year), Singapore and Ningbo-Zhoushan — and seven of the world's top ten are in China.
cards.extra: Rotterdam is Europe's busiest. The centre of gravity of global shipping sits firmly in East Asia.

cards.front: Name the world's key maritime chokepoints.
cards.back: The Strait of Hormuz (Gulf oil), the Strait of Malacca (Asia–Europe trade), the Suez Canal (Europe–Asia shortcut), Bab-el-Mandeb (Red Sea), the Turkish Straits/Bosphorus (the only way out of the Black Sea) and the Panama Canal (Atlantic–Pacific).
cards.extra: Block any one — like the 2021 container ship that wedged across the Suez Canal — and shockwaves run through the whole global economy; when Suez is unusable, ships divert the long way round the Cape of Good Hope. (Cross-links the Geography cards.)

cards.front: Who are the world's biggest exporters of goods?
cards.back: China (#1 by far — 'the world's factory'), then the United States and Germany.
cards.extra: Germany is Europe's export powerhouse (cars, machinery, chemicals); the US leads in services and high-tech goods.

cards.front: Why is so much of the world's merchant shipping registered in Panama, Liberia and the Marshall Islands?
cards.back: 'Flags of convenience' — ship owners register vessels in these countries for lower taxes, cheaper crews and lighter regulation, even though the owners are elsewhere.
cards.extra: It's why three small states top the list of shipping registries. (Cross-links Panama, Liberia and the Marshall Islands in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: Which chokepoint controls all access to the Black Sea — and why does it matter now?
cards.back: The Turkish Straits — the Bosphorus and the Dardanelles, running through Istanbul. They are the only sea route between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean.
cards.extra: Whoever holds them controls Russia's and Ukraine's access to the open ocean — vital for Black Sea grain exports and for the Russian navy. Turkey governs passage under the 1936 Montreux Convention. (Cross-links Türkiye in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: When the Suez Canal is unusable, which long detour do ships take instead?
cards.back: Around the Cape of Good Hope, the southern tip of Africa — adding roughly 10 days and ~6,000 km to an Asia–Europe voyage.
cards.extra: Exactly what happened from late 2023, when Houthi attacks in the Red Sea pushed most shipping off the Suez route and around Africa, raising freight costs worldwide. Before Suez opened in 1869, the Cape was the only sea route from Europe to Asia. (Cross-links South Africa and Egypt in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: Which strait is the world's busiest trade chokepoint — and what is the 'Malacca Dilemma'?
cards.back: The Strait of Malacca, between Malaysia, Indonesia and Singapore — the main gate between the Indian and Pacific Oceans. Around a quarter of all seaborne trade and ~80% of China's oil imports pass through it.
cards.extra: The 'Malacca Dilemma' is Beijing's fear that a rival (the US) could choke that flow in a crisis — a key reason China builds overland pipelines and its Indian-Ocean 'string of pearls'. (Cross-links Malaysia, Indonesia and Singapore in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: Which narrow strait guards the southern entrance to the Red Sea and the Suez route?
cards.back: Bab-el-Mandeb — the 'Gate of Tears', between Yemen and Djibouti/Eritrea. Almost everything sailing to or from the Suez Canal must pass through it.
cards.extra: That makes it the soft underbelly of the Europe–Asia sea lane: Houthi missile and drone attacks from Yemen in 2023–24 drove much shipping off the Red Sea entirely, around the Cape of Good Hope. (Cross-links Yemen and Djibouti in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: Why is the Suez Canal so important — and how fragile is it?
cards.back: Opened in 1869, it links the Mediterranean to the Red Sea, sparing ships the long trip around Africa. Roughly 12–15% of world trade passes through it.
cards.extra: A single ship can paralyse it — the grounded Ever Given blocked it for six days in 2021, stalling ~$10bn of trade a day. Transit tolls are a major source of revenue for Egypt. (Cross-links Egypt in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: What does the Panama Canal connect — and why did it recently have to ration traffic?
cards.back: It links the Atlantic and Pacific across Central America (opened 1914), sparing ships the long voyage around South America — vital for US coast-to-coast and US–Asia trade.
cards.extra: Unlike Suez, it lifts ships over the isthmus using freshwater locks fed by rain, so a 2023–24 drought forced it to cut daily crossings. The US built and long ran it, handing control to Panama in 1999. (Cross-links Panama in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: Beyond China's mega-ports, which ports are the great regional gateways and transshipment hubs?
cards.back: Singapore (the model transshipment hub, beside the Malacca Strait), Rotterdam (Europe's gateway), Jebel Ali in Dubai (the Middle East's hub), Los Angeles–Long Beach (the main US gateway for Asian imports) and Busan (Northeast Asia).
cards.extra: A transshipment hub is where containers change ships rather than entering the country — much of what passes through Singapore is never 'Singaporean'. (Cross-links Singapore, the UAE and the Netherlands in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: What new shipping route is opening as the Arctic warms — and why does it matter?
cards.back: The Northern Sea Route, along Russia's Arctic coast. As sea ice retreats it can cut an Asia–Europe voyage by up to ~40% versus the Suez route.
cards.extra: Still seasonal, ice-bound much of the year and largely Russian-controlled — but warming is slowly making it viable, a rare case of climate change redrawing the map of trade and great-power rivalry. (Cross-links the Global Trends climate card and Russia in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: Beyond commercial ports, which overseas bases show where great powers project naval power?
cards.back: They cluster on chokepoints. Djibouti, on Bab-el-Mandeb, hosts US, Chinese, French and Japanese bases side by side — China's first overseas base (2017) is there.
cards.extra: Other key nodes: the US Fifth Fleet at Bahrain (the Gulf), Britain's Gibraltar and Diego Garcia, and China's 'string of pearls' of ports across the Indian Ocean. (Cross-links Djibouti and Bahrain in the Geography decks.)

cards.front: Where are the world's most advanced computer chips made?
cards.back: Overwhelmingly in Taiwan, by one company — TSMC — which makes about 90% of the most advanced chips and ~65% of all made-to-order ('foundry') chips.
cards.extra: This concentration makes Taiwan a unique geopolitical pressure point — its 'Silicon Shield'. (Cross-links Taiwan in the East Asia deck.)

cards.front: One Dutch company is a choke point for the entire chip industry — which, and why?
cards.back: ASML — the only company on Earth that makes EUV lithography machines, the tools needed to print the most advanced chips.
cards.extra: No EUV machine, no cutting-edge chip — which is why blocking ASML sales to China is a front line of the US–China tech war.

cards.front: Which country dominates 'rare earths' and critical minerals — and why does it matter?
cards.back: China — it does roughly 90% of the world's rare-earth refining (and processes most other critical minerals too).
cards.extra: Rare earths go into the magnets for EVs, wind turbines, electronics and guided missiles — so refining dominance is a powerful economic weapon China can switch on and off.

cards.front: How does almost all intercontinental internet traffic actually travel?
cards.back: Through fibre-optic cables lying on the ocean floor — about 99% of international data, not satellites.
cards.extra: A few hundred undersea cables carry the world's communications and finance; cutting or tapping them is a real strategic vulnerability.

cards.front: Summarise the advanced-chip supply chain — and why no one country controls it.
cards.back: Designs often come from the US (e.g. Nvidia), the machines from the Netherlands (ASML), and the manufacturing from Taiwan (TSMC) — a deeply interdependent global chain.
cards.extra: That interdependence is both a source of efficiency and the central vulnerability of the modern economy.

cards.front: Which companies are the world's most valuable — and what does that reveal?
cards.back: Mostly US technology giants — Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, Alphabet (Google) and Amazon — plus Saudi Aramco (oil).
cards.extra: The 21st-century 'commanding heights' are tech and energy; Nvidia's surge to the top reflects the AI boom built on its chips.

cards.front: Has the share of people in extreme poverty (below $2.15/day) got better, worse, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Better
cards.extra: 47% in 1981 → 10% in 2024. The most important fact about the modern world: extreme poverty has collapsed — it roughly halved in the two decades to 2017. Most people wrongly think it has risen or stayed flat.

viz.label: the share of people in extreme poverty (below $2.15/day)
viz.why: The most important fact about the modern world: extreme poverty has collapsed — it roughly halved in the two decades to 2017. Most people wrongly think it has risen or stayed flat.

cards.front: Has the child (under-5) mortality rate got better, worse, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Better
cards.extra: 94 per 1,000 births in 1990 → 37 per 1,000 births in 2024. Fewer children die than ever before — a proxy for the whole of human development (health, nutrition, water, education). When children survive, families choose to have fewer of them.

viz.label: the child (under-5) mortality rate
viz.why: Fewer children die than ever before — a proxy for the whole of human development (health, nutrition, water, education). When children survive, families choose to have fewer of them.

cards.front: Has life expectancy at birth got better, worse, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Better
cards.extra: 51 years in 1960 → 73 years in 2024. Average human lifespan has risen by more than 20 years since 1960 — the gain is global, not just rich countries.

viz.label: life expectancy at birth
viz.why: Average human lifespan has risen by more than 20 years since 1960 — the gain is global, not just rich countries.

cards.front: Has the number of babies per woman (fertility) risen, fallen, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Fallen
cards.extra: 4.7 in 1960 → 2.2 in 2024. 'Peak child': as child survival and incomes rise and girls are educated, families shrink toward ~2 children — which is why world population growth is slowing, not exploding.

viz.label: the number of babies per woman (fertility)
viz.why: 'Peak child': as child survival and incomes rise and girls are educated, families shrink toward ~2 children — which is why world population growth is slowing, not exploding.

cards.front: Has the share of people with access to electricity got better, worse, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Better
cards.extra: 73% in 1998 → 92% in 2023. Access to electricity keeps climbing toward universal — a quiet, steady improvement that rarely makes the news.

viz.label: the share of people with access to electricity
viz.why: Access to electricity keeps climbing toward universal — a quiet, steady improvement that rarely makes the news.

cards.front: Has the share of people with at least basic drinking water got better, worse, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Better
cards.extra: 81% in 2000 → 91% in 2024. Basic services keep spreading; the share without safe water keeps shrinking.

viz.label: the share of people with at least basic drinking water
viz.why: Basic services keep spreading; the share without safe water keeps shrinking.

cards.front: Has the share of people using the internet got better, worse, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Better
cards.extra: 16% in 2005 → 71% in 2024. From a rich-world novelty to most of humanity in two decades — one of the fastest technology rollouts in history.

viz.label: the share of people using the internet
viz.why: From a rich-world novelty to most of humanity in two decades — one of the fastest technology rollouts in history.

cards.front: Has the share of children vaccinated against measles got better, worse, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Better
cards.extra: 13% in 1980 → 84% in 2024. Vaccination coverage rose from a minority of the world's children to the great majority.

viz.label: the share of children vaccinated against measles
viz.why: Vaccination coverage rose from a minority of the world's children to the great majority.

cards.front: Has the share of people who are undernourished got better, worse, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Better
cards.extra: 13% in 2001 → 8% in 2023. Hunger has fallen over the long run (with recent setbacks from conflict and the pandemic).

viz.label: the share of people who are undernourished
viz.why: Hunger has fallen over the long run (with recent setbacks from conflict and the pandemic).

cards.front: Has the share of people living in cities risen, fallen, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Risen
cards.extra: 34% in 1960 → 58% in 2024. Humanity crossed the line to majority-urban around 2007 — a profound shift in how we live.

viz.label: the share of people living in cities
viz.why: Humanity crossed the line to majority-urban around 2007 — a profound shift in how we live.

cards.front: Has global greenhouse-gas emissions got better, worse, or stayed about the same?
cards.back: Worse
cards.extra: 23,229 Mt CO2e in 1970 → 53,206 Mt CO2e in 2024. THE HONEST EXCEPTION. Most human-development measures are improving — but greenhouse-gas emissions and the climate are getting worse. A fact-based worldview holds both at once: the world can be 'better' AND still 'bad'.

viz.label: global greenhouse-gas emissions
viz.why: THE HONEST EXCEPTION. Most human-development measures are improving — but greenhouse-gas emissions and the climate are getting worse. A fact-based worldview holds both at once: the world can be 'better' AND still 'bad'.

cards.front: Overall, is the world getting better, worse, or staying about the same?
cards.back: On almost every measure of human development — poverty, child mortality, life expectancy, education, basic services — dramatically BETTER over recent decades.
cards.extra: The big exception is the environment (CO2 and climate), which is getting worse. A fact-based worldview holds both: the world can be 'better' and still 'bad'.

cards.front: Why do most people get these facts wrong — and almost always too pessimistically?
cards.back: Hans Rosling's 'dramatic instincts': the GAP instinct (imagining a rich/poor binary), the NEGATIVITY instinct (bad news travels; gradual improvement is invisible), and the STRAIGHT-LINE instinct (assuming trends keep going straight).
cards.extra: On Gapminder's fact quiz, the average person scores WORSE than random chance — 'worse than chimpanzees' — because our mental model of the world is decades out of date.

cards.front: Rosling replaced 'developed vs developing' with four income Levels — what are they?
cards.back: Level 1 (under ~$2/day), Level 2 (~$2–8/day), Level 3 (~$8–32/day), Level 4 (over ~$32/day). Roughly 1 / 3 / 2 / 1 billion people live on each.
cards.extra: Most of humanity lives in the MIDDLE (Levels 2–3), not split into a poor 'them' and a rich 'us' — that binary is the gap instinct, and it's wrong.

cards.front: What best predicts how many children a woman has — religion, culture, or something else?
cards.back: Income and girls' education, above all. As incomes rise, children survive, and girls are schooled, family size falls toward ~2 — across every religion and region.
cards.extra: This is why the population 'explosion' is ending: we are near 'peak child'.

cards.front: Why is Taiwan the world's most dangerous flashpoint?
cards.back: Three layers stack on one island: the Chinese civil war never formally ended (the losing Nationalists fled there in 1949, and Beijing regards the island as unfinished business); it became a prosperous democracy under informal US protection; and TSMC makes ~90% of the world's most advanced chips there — so a war would collide the two superpowers AND cut the supply of the commodity the modern economy runs on.
cards.extra: The chip concentration is called Taiwan's 'Silicon Shield' — an attack would wound the attacker too. The US keeps 'strategic ambiguity': arming Taiwan without promising to fight. (Pulls together East Asia, the History Chinese-revolution card, and the Technology cards.)

cards.front: Venezuela sits on the world's largest proven oil reserves. Why is it poor?
cards.back: Because resources are not wealth — institutions turn one into the other. State takeover and mismanagement of the oil industry hollowed out production, oil-price collapse removed the cushion, and money-printing tipped into hyperinflation; millions emigrated. Reserves stayed in the ground while the economy that could pump them dissolved.
cards.extra: The textbook 'resource curse': oil wealth let the state skip building the boring machinery (taxes, courts, industry) that actually sustains prosperity. (Pulls together South America, the Economics hyperinflation card, and the Energy reserves-vs-production card.)

cards.front: How did Singapore — a resourceless island expelled by Malaysia in 1965 — become one of the richest countries on Earth?
cards.back: Location plus institutions. It sits beside the Strait of Malacca, the busiest trade chokepoint on Earth — and under Lee Kuan Yew it added what the location alone couldn't: rule of law, clean government, open trade and a world-class port, turning a transit point into the model entrepôt (trade, finance, logistics).
cards.extra: The mirror image of Venezuela: no resources but strong institutions, versus vast resources and broken ones. Geography deals the hand; policy plays it. (Pulls together Southeast Asia, the Trade & Seas Malacca and transshipment cards, and the Economics deck.)

cards.front: Why is one Korean peninsula home to both Samsung and famine?
cards.back: It's history's cleanest natural experiment. One people, one geography, one culture was split at the 38th parallel in 1945 and frozen by the 1950–53 war. The South chose export capitalism under a US security umbrella and became a rich democracy; the North chose a closed dynastic command economy and became a poor garrison state with nuclear weapons.
cards.extra: Same inputs, opposite institutions, opposite outcomes — the strongest single piece of evidence that institutions, not geography or culture, drive prosperity. (Pulls together East Asia, the History Korean-War card, and the Economics big ideas.)

cards.front: How does Panama — one small country — sit at the centre of world shipping twice over?
cards.back: Once by geography, once by law. The canal (opened 1914, US-built, handed over in 1999) is the only shortcut between the Atlantic and Pacific; and Panama's ship registry is the world's largest, because owners everywhere register under its 'flag of convenience'. It even runs on the US dollar.
cards.extra: A country can matter through what crosses it rather than what it makes. (Pulls together Central America, the Trade & Seas Panama-Canal and flags-of-convenience cards, and the Economics dollar cards.)

cards.front: How did landlocked, mountain-locked Switzerland end up among the richest countries on Earth?
cards.back: By selling what wars destroy: trust. Mountains made it defensible, permanent neutrality (since 1815) kept it out of both world wars, and that stability became the product — banking, insurance, pharmaceuticals, precision industry — while the empires around it burned their capital twice in thirty years.
cards.extra: The great exception to 'landlocked is a handicap' — institutions overrode geography. (Pulls together the Western Europe overview's landlocked card, the History world-war cards, and the Economics banking cards.)

cards.front: How did the Gulf states turn oil into skylines, airlines and ports — and why the rush to diversify?
cards.back: Hydrocarbon rents were piped into sovereign wealth funds and infrastructure meant to outlive the oil: Dubai built Jebel Ali (the Middle East's great port) and a global airline hub; Saudi Arabia's 'Vision 2030' bets the surplus on a post-oil economy. The deadline is the energy transition itself — they can see the horizon on their main export.
cards.extra: Dubai is the proof it can work: oil is now a small share of its economy. (Pulls together the Middle East deck, the Energy transition card, and the Trade & Seas Jebel Ali card.)

cards.front: Why did Britain, the Soviet Union and the United States all fail in Afghanistan?
cards.back: The country resists central control: mountain terrain fragments it into valley communities, the population is an ethnic patchwork with loyalties older than the state, neighbours (above all Pakistan) sponsor factions, and every occupier eventually leaves — so locals wait them out. There was never a functioning central state to hand power back to.
cards.extra: 'The graveyard of empires' — Britain lost three wars there, the Soviet exit (1989) helped crack the USSR, and the US left in 2021 after twenty years. (Pulls together South Asia, the History Soviet-Afghan and 9/11-era cards, and the Cold-War proxy pattern.)

cards.front: For most of recorded history, China and India were the world's largest economies. What happened around 1800?
cards.back: The Industrial Revolution — coal, machines, applied science and commercial institutions, first in Britain then Europe and America — made Western economies grow explosively while Asia's stood still. That 'Great Divergence' built the world of rich West / poor rest that Rosling's income levels describe.
cards.extra: Today's 'rise of Asia' is largely a *reversion* to the old normal, not an anomaly. (Pulls together the Economics growth ideas, the History arc, and the Global Trends income-levels cards.)

cards.front: Why does Latin America speak Spanish, half of Africa French — and why are so many African borders dead straight?
cards.back: Because today's map is the fossil record of European empire. Colonisers exported their languages, law and religion; and at the 1884–85 Berlin Conference the powers drew Africa's borders with rulers in a room in Europe — ignoring the peoples on the ground, which is why so many post-independence states hold rival nations inside arbitrary lines.
cards.extra: Decolonisation freed the states but kept the borders (17 countries in 1960 alone — the 'Year of Africa'). (Pulls together the Geography language & independence cards, and the History decolonisation era.)

cards.front: Which power ran the world before America — and what single week made the handoff undeniable?
cards.back: Britain — the Royal Navy ruled the seas, sterling was the world's money, and the empire was history's largest. Two world wars spent that down, and the 1956 Suez Crisis made it public: when Britain and France seized the canal, Washington ordered them out — and they went.
cards.extra: The financial handoff had already happened at Bretton Woods (1944), where the dollar replaced the pound as the anchor. Hegemony and reserve currency travel together. (Pulls together the History Suez card, the Economics reserve-currency cards, and Geopolitics.)

cards.front: Why do so many 20th-century wars — Korea, Vietnam, Cuba, Afghanistan — fit a single pattern?
cards.back: They were proxy wars: the US and USSR could not fight directly (mutually assured destruction made that suicidal), so their rivalry was fought through local conflicts — each superpower arming a side, each local war becoming a square on the global chessboard.
cards.extra: That's why the Cold War stayed 'cold' at the centre and hot at the edges — and why so many of the History deck's events cluster into one system. India led the states refusing to pick a side (non-alignment). (Pulls together the History Cold-War era and the Hard Power deterrence/MAD cards.)

cards.front: How did China go from the 'century of humiliation' to superpower within one lifetime?
cards.back: Four acts: humiliation (Opium Wars to Japanese occupation — carved up by foreign powers); unification under Mao in 1949 (sovereign again, but impoverished by his campaigns); Deng Xiaoping's 1978 opening (market reforms that turned it into the world's factory — biggest exporter, 7 of the 10 busiest ports); and now the conversion of that wealth into hard power — a blue-water navy, a chip drive, the fastest-growing nuclear arsenal.
cards.extra: The one story stitching together more of this system's cards than any other. (Pulls together East Asia, three History China cards, and the Geopolitics ports, exporters and warhead cards.)

cards.front: What is the demographic transition — and where is each world region on it?
cards.back: As countries develop, child mortality falls first and fertility follows (the world: ~4.7 children per woman in the 1960s → ~2.2 today), so population growth slows and eventually stops. East Asia and Europe are already aging; Africa is still young and growing — which is where much of this century's workforce and growth story will happen.
cards.extra: The world has likely passed 'peak child' — the number of children has stopped growing; population still rises only because past generations are living longer. (Pulls together the Global Trends fertility and peak-child cards and the Geography population cards.)

cards.front: Why is English the world's language?
cards.back: Two empires in a row spoke it. Britain spread English across a quarter of the planet (North America, India, Africa, Oceania); then the United States — the next hegemon — made it the language of business, science, aviation and the internet. Once enough of the world used it, network effects locked it in: you learn English because everyone else did.
cards.extra: The same handoff as the reserve currency, in linguistic form. (Pulls together the Geography language cards, the History empire arc, and the Britain→America thread.)

cards.front: Why does so much of today's world — the UN veto, the IMF, the dollar, even the nuclear club — trace back to a single year?
cards.back: Because 1945's victors built the world in their image and institutions outlive the moment that made them: the UN Security Council's permanent five ARE the WWII winners; Bretton Woods (1944) made the dollar the anchor of world finance; and the NPT later froze the nuclear club around the same five states.
cards.extra: The master key of this whole system: when the same names keep appearing at every top table, 1945 is usually the reason. (Pulls together the World Overview institutions, the History post-war order, the Economics Bretton Woods card, and the Hard Power P5/NPT cards.)

cards.front: Why can the United States borrow almost without limit, sanction almost anyone, and print the world's money?
cards.back: Because the dollar is the world's reserve currency (~58% of global reserves): commodities are priced in it, trade is settled through it, and most international payments touch US banks — so Washington can cut a country out of world finance without firing a shot, and the world's savings queue up to fund US debt ('exorbitant privilege').
cards.extra: Built at Bretton Woods, survived Nixon ending gold convertibility in 1971. Challengers (the yuan, BRICS talk) remain marginal so far. (Pulls together the Economics reserve-currency and Bretton Woods cards and the Geopolitics deck.)

cards.front: What problem was the European Union actually invented to solve?
cards.back: The German question. France and Germany fought three wars in seventy years (1870, 1914, 1939); the 1951 Coal and Steel Community placed the raw materials of war under joint control, deliberately making another war 'materially impossible' — and that peace project grew into the single market, the EU and the euro.
cards.extra: It worked: war between France and Germany is now unthinkable. The economics was always the means; peace was the point. (Pulls together the Western Europe deck, the History post-war cards, and the Economics deck.)

cards.front: Why did the two countries that LOST World War II become economic superpowers?
cards.back: Because the winners needed them. To anchor the Cold War, the US gave Japan and West Germany a security umbrella, open access to its markets and (imposed) pacifist constitutions — so their talent and capital flowed into industry instead of armies, and within a generation the defeated were out-exporting the victors.
cards.extra: The 1945 order made losing well better than winning badly. Both are now rearming as that order frays — Germany is already a top-five military spender. (Pulls together the History post-war cards, the Geopolitics spending card, and the Economics deck.)

cards.front: Why is Iran at the centre of so many crises at once?
cards.back: Four kinds of leverage stack on one country: geography (it holds the north shore of Hormuz, the strait a fifth of the world's oil passes through); revolution (since 1979 it defines itself against the US-led order); religion (it is the Shia power, rival to Sunni Saudi Arabia, fighting through proxies); and the nuclear file (its programme is the NPT's central worry).
cards.extra: Remove any one layer and Iran is a regional player; together they make it a global pressure point. (Pulls together the Middle East deck, the History Iranian-Revolution card, the Energy Hormuz card, and the Hard Power NPT card.)

cards.front: Why is the India–Pakistan border one of the world's most dangerous?
cards.back: A 1947 decision still armed with warheads: Partition split British India along religious lines amid massacre and one of history's largest displacements, left Kashmir disputed, and seeded three wars — and both states then built nuclear arsenals *outside* the NPT, facing each other across a live border.
cards.extra: The only place two nuclear powers regularly exchange fire. (Pulls together the History Partition card, the South Asia deck, and the Hard Power nuclear cards.)

cards.front: What does geography explain about Russian behaviour — from the tsars to Ukraine?
cards.back: Russia's core is a flat plain with no natural defensive line (Napoleon 1812, Hitler 1941 both marched straight in), and its navy has no free exit — every route out passes a bottleneck someone else controls (the Turkish Straits, the Baltic narrows) or freezes. Hence the centuries-long push for buffer zones and warm-water ports: Crimea and Sevastopol, resistance to NATO on its plain, the obsession with the 'near abroad'.
cards.extra: Geography explains the anxiety; it doesn't excuse the invasions. (Pulls together the Geography Russia cards, the History invasions and 2022 card, and the Turkish-Straits chokepoint card.)

cards.front: Why are semiconductors called 'the new oil' — and who controls what?
cards.back: Because everything advanced now runs on them (AI, weapons, cars, phones) and their supply chain has chokepoints the way oil has straits: the US controls the designs (Nvidia), one Dutch firm the machines (ASML — 100% of EUV), one Taiwanese firm the advanced manufacturing (TSMC ~90%), and China ~90% of rare-earth refining. Export controls have become what naval blockades were to oil.
cards.extra: The US–China tech war is fought along exactly these four chokepoints. (Pulls together the Technology cards, the Taiwan thread, and the Hard Power deck.)

cards.front: How does the climate fight redraw the geopolitical map?
cards.back: Decarbonisation moves leverage from those who pump hydrocarbons to those who control the replacement: lithium (Chile, Bolivia, Australia), rare-earth refining and battery/solar manufacturing (China, dominantly) — while oil states race to diversify before demand peaks, and the melting Arctic opens a new Russian-controlled trade route. Meanwhile emissions themselves are still rising.
cards.extra: The honest double truth: the transition is accelerating AND the problem is still getting worse — the Global Trends exception. (Pulls together the Energy transition card, the Global Trends climate card, the Technology minerals card, and the Arctic-route card.)

cards.front: Why has Israel–Palestine defied solution for over 75 years?
cards.back: Two peoples claim the same small land, and every layer of the conflict is load-bearing: Israel's 1948 founding was independence for one side and displacement (the Nakba) for the other; the 1967 war left millions of Palestinians under occupation; and the final-status questions — Jerusalem, settlements, refugees, security — are ones neither side's politics can concede. Outside powers arm and fund the sides, making it a regional fault line, not just a local one.
cards.extra: Peace processes (Camp David, Oslo) traded land for peace with Egypt and Jordan but broke down on the Palestinian core questions. (Pulls together the Middle East deck, the History 1948 card, and the Iran-proxy thread.)
